OpenAI whistleblower William Saunders is testifying before a Senate subcommittee today (so is Helen Toner and Margaret Mitchell). His written testimony is online now. Here are the most important parts 🧵 https://t.co/aGOZCFHf7j

Saunders, like many others at the top AI companies, think artificial general intelligence (AGI) could come in “as little as three years.” He cites OpenAI's new o1 model, which has surpassed human experts in some challenging technical benchmarks for the first time... https://t.co/eYSanNptuz

OpenAI has “repeatedly prioritized deployment over rigor. I believe there is a real risk they will miss important dangerous capabilities in future AI systems.”...

Written testimony: https://t.co/7wXcUJa0ZR

Live video: 
https://t.co/2ua0Bzzgmb https://t.co/zVUfaRwTAE

“When I was at OpenAI, there were long periods of time where there were vulnerabilities that would have allowed me or hundreds of other engineers at the company to bypass access controls and steal the company’s most advanced AI systems including GPT-4.”

This is consistent... https://t.co/85R5wpzoSU
with claims made by Leopold Aschenbrenner, who alleges OpenAI found a pretense to fire him after he complained about cybersecurity vulnerabilities to the board: https://t.co/pM5rUF9EgU

There was a large hack at OpenAI last year. We only found out about it recently...

https://t.co/p7zu8BmSYL https://t.co/mTvocMDnPJ
“No one knows how to ensure that AGI systems will be safe and controlled.” Not news to anyone following this field, but an important point to convey to the Senate... https://t.co/MNKrSu7CF8

“I and other employees who resigned doubt they will be ready in time. This is true not just with OpenAI; the incentives to prioritize rapid development apply to the entire industry. This is why a policy response is needed.”

What does he suggest? ... https://t.co/cLhweHf2Pv
Familiar things (that still don't exist): whistleblower protections for AI employees, mandatory 3rd party testing and result-sharing... https://t.co/fSqznAadg9

“I resigned from OpenAI because I lost faith that by themselves they will make responsible decisions about AGI.” 

Saunders also includes the full text of the Right to Warn open letter signed by him and other AI employees and alums: https://t.co/2f9QgahNN8 https://t.co/7lkugxBvOt

We spend a lot of time debating what Sam Altman and other AI CEOs believe deep down. Idt we really know, but it's hard to dispute that people like Saunders (and others like Geoffrey Hinton) are speaking from a place of genuine concern about the risks posed by AI...

Ofc, there are abundant examples of people close to AI massively overhyping its capabilities and trajectory, but the argument people often make in reply is: there's an asymmetry in being wrong. The costs of underrating the tech are so much greater (possibly existential)...

than the costs of overrating it (regulating prematurely/too heavily; not focusing on other harms). 

The rejoinder is that this argument proves too much. It could be used to prevent any emerging technology, even if the benefits clearly outweigh the risks. Some AI boosters...

are really worried that AI could be like nuclear power: over-feared and under-utilized. 

I think all you can really do is engage deeply with the best arguments for each of these positions and decide for yourself.

Here are all of Gavin Newsom's comments related to SB 1047 from his convo with Marc Benioff at the Dreamforce conference on 9/17/24 (I created a Salesforce account so you don't have to):

Benioff: What's been the most difficult thing that you've had to decide on?

Newsom: I mean, look, I think there's one bill that is sort of outsized in terms of public discourse and consciousness. It's SB 1047. There are dozens and dozens of other bills that are more surgical. Bills as it relates to AI, deepfakes more of the traditional issues.
---
Benioff: How do you think it will affect our customers?

Newsom: That’s an open-ended question.
---
Newsom: And we've been working over the course of the last couple of years to come up with some rational regulation that supports risk-taking, but not recklessness.

That supports the ecosystem that's so unique and vibrant here in California and does put us at a competitive disadvantage, but at the same time puts the rules of the road at hand.

That's challenging now in this space, particularly with SB 1047, because of the sort of outsized impact that legislation could have and the chilling effect, particularly in the open source community, that legislation could have. So I'm processing that in consideration more broadly of what are demonstrable risks in AI and what are the hypothetical risks. Can't solve for everything, what can we solve for?
---
Newsom: Look, I'm not naive, Marc and I talk about this all the time, California's competitiveness vis-a-vis the other states. The world that we have met constantly is competing against us and we've got to step up our game.
---
Newsom: And this is a space where we dominate and I want to maintain our dominance. I want to maintain our innovation. I want to maintain our ecosystem. I want to continue to lead.

At the same time, you feel a deep sense of responsibility to address some of those more extreme concerns that I think many of us have, even the biggest and strongest promoters of this technology have.

And that's a difficult place to land when you have a finality of a bill where that bill is locked.

I’m seeing lots of claims that the NYT x-rays showing children with bullets lodged in their brains are obviously fake.

I’m not a radiologist or a ballistics expert, but I wanted to offer some context/thoughts. (FWIW, I also thought the x-rays seemed fishy at first.)

1. Some have claimed that NYT does not fact check opinion essays, but this is not true. I just published one with them, and it was by far the most extensive fact-checking process I’ve gone through. Given the sensitivity of this topic, I’d imagine the process was even more thorough here.
2. NYT opinion is separate from its reporting and my impression is that firewall is taken very seriously.
3. There are lots of people who can claim some plausible-sounding expertise on this topic, some of them dispute the veracity of the x-rays, and Israel defenders and the algo elevate those perspectives (ofc the inverse is true to some extent, the algo wants to increase engagement, and inflammatory stuff does the trick). In this thread (from someone who I personally like), the tweets in support are all clearly from partisans on this issue: https://t.co/rTcKWpCOrX
Seriously, just look at the cited tweets, they’re all from partisans on this issue. One is from someone whose bio is “KEEP ATTACKING”, one who dismisses the op-ed bc 90% of the medical professionals are Muslim, one says “forever a soldier” in bio.
4. I looked for some other images of CT scout scans of bullets lodged in someone’s head, and they look pretty similar to the ones in the NYT, e.g. https://t.co/AwIw3qjFmK, https://t.co/USbq1C9bCl
5. The author of the piece wrote a reply to some of this criticism that addresses the fake image claim: https://t.co/mDFmrFeryL
Here’s the relevant part in full: “3. What you mean by "multiple forensic ballistic specialists" is "multiple people claiming on Twitter to be forensic ballistic specialists". I'm a trauma surgeon and I'm board certified in neurocritical care, I trea people who've been shot in the head on a daily basis. I did my residency at the busiest trauma center in New England and my trauma/critical care fellowship at the only level 1 trauma center for all of southern New Jersey, located in the middle of Camden. I assure you that the CT scout films that Dr. Syed provided in the piece are entirely typical of someone who has been shot in the head but is still alive. That's something that people commenting don't seem to understand: if the bullet caused massive cavitation to the point of destroying even the scalp and obliterating a large part of the skull then nobody would call a doctor or get a CT scan for that patient, especially in Gaza, because they're obviously dead. They would be sent to the morgue from the emergency department. So, just like in the United States or anywhere else, physicians saw and did investigations on patients whose injuries were life-threatening but who were not obviously dead on arrival.”
6. Many of the responses go something along the lines of: deliberate killing of children goes against the interests of Israel/IDF so therefore wouldn’t happen. (e.g. https://t.co/8Ox4Jm0qYm) By extension, Abu Ghraib didn’t happen. Militaries in war act against their interests all the time. You don’t need to believe there is a coordinated/deliberate effort to kill children in Gaza to believe that some IDF soldiers are deliberately killing children because they think they can get away with it and/or harbor some genocidal intent. Given the post Oct-7 rhetoric coming from senior members of the Israeli govt and military, this shouldn’t be surprising.

I spent nearly three months reporting on California’s first-of-its-kind AI safety bill, SB 1047 and consolidated all my coverage here, for the SB 1047 completionist or someone just learning about it now.

Even though the bill is dead, I think the fight over it is a... https://t.co/DTOb9OV85a

really instructive preview into the future of AI politics and maybe even politics more broadly.

It was thrilling and utterly exhausting to cover an ongoing political fight, which grew to truly epic proportions...

https://t.co/i4StLCWzPd

especially for state level legislation (e.g. Nancy Pelosi opposed the bill, and Elon Musk endorsed it).

I worked basically every weekend and most nights from early August until the bill was vetoed...
I was fortunate to write for a bunch of publications for the first time, like The New York Times, The Verge, TIME, Thomson Reuters Foundation, SF Standard, The American Prospect, and Transformer...

In many ways, I was most proud of my piece in The Nation, which kicked off my reporting on the bill. I think my headline and subtitle capture the central takeaway: “California’s AI Safety Bill Is a Mask-Off Moment for the Industry...
AI’s top industrialists say they want regulation—until someone tries to regulate them.” ...
My reporting on SB 1047 also got shared by some of the most prominent figures in AI, including Nobel laureate Geoffrey Hinton, noted AI critic Gary Marcus, and Life 3.0 author Max Tegmark...
I was also excited to see my SB 1047 reporting and analysis referenced in NYT, The Atlantic, Axios, The Brookings Institution, The LA Times, Fortune, and Vox...

Now that I've had a chance to recover and breathe a bit, I plan to publish more frequently to my Substack (now called "The Obsolete Newsletter," to coincide with the book I'm writing on capitalism, geopolitics, and AI.)

I just published an op-ed in @GuardianUS on why @GavinNewsom vetoed California's AI safety bill (SB 1047) despite overwhelming public support. 🧵 https://t.co/hs9nkllbOE

The bill was "controversial" in the sense that it generated a lot of opposition from tech and political elites. But it was not very controversial with Californians!

https://t.co/J9B6b34Rz4 https://t.co/K2SewLHMm1
So why did Newsom veto such a popular bill? In short: intense and effective lobbying from the AI industry and its allies in Congress.

8 CA Democrats in Congress, including @RepZoeLofgren, @RepAnnaEshoo, and @RoKhanna, asked Newsom to veto.

These 3 have taken over $4M from the tech industry - nearly half their lifetime top donations (note that this does include employee contributions).

The final blow likely came from @SpeakerPelosi, who cited the congressional letter in her own statement against the bill. Her 2023 financial disclosure shows her husband owns $16-80M in Amazon, Google, Microsoft and Nvidia.
Her spokesperson told me: “Speaker Pelosi does not own any stocks, and she has no prior knowledge or subsequent involvement in any transactions.”
This divide reveals a stark reality: in Silicon Valley and Congress, AI is seen as a ticket to fortune and power. But for average voters, it's more a source of fear and uncertainty.

As Sunny Gandhi from @EncodeJustice told me: "This isn't just a policy disagreement; it's a moral chasm between Silicon Valley and Main Street."

Newsom's veto shows which of those he values more.

Read the full piece here:

I actually think it's largely a myth that VC-backed founders are these brave, risk-absorbing people. It's even more overstated than it is for VCs (who take less risk than founders but I think people kinda realize that already). If you found a company and get VC-backing 🧵 https://t.co/VEvAX2yosg

you can pay yourself a nice salary, you get an enormous amount of social status (esp in the Bay), and even if the company goes bust, you'll probably have way better job oppos after than you would have if you stayed in a normal job

https://t.co/AyAItU8Yoy

(barring you being involved in some scandal, and even then it might not matter).

The CEO of the startup I worked at bought a house in the Hamptons. Were we profitable? Not even close. He probably just paid himself a nice salary for many years.

If we went bust, his oppos would be incredible given the network and experience he got through the job.

So who bears the risk?

The employees. They're usually working for less than they could make at a Big Tech firm, w/ longer hours and more turbulence and turnover.

They're compensated with equity, but in most cases, this will never be worth anything. You also typically have to exercise your options around when you leave, which can cost many thousands, with no guaranteed payoff.
You may end up getting more work experience faster than you would at a larger company, which can lead to better exit oppos, but the benefit is not nearly as dramatic as it is for the founders.
If you're a founder putting all of your own money into the company with no external backing, you actually are doing something very gutsy. But that's not what we're talking about here.

It may even be socially desirable to have things work this way, where VCs defray risk in such a way that it becomes rational for more people to try something that's likely to fail, but can we dispel the myth that founders are somehow not getting a sweet deal here?
(Though the VC model can also incentivize investments in companies that create large externalities and risks to society. It creates another powerful anti-regulatory actor, e.g. Andreessen Horowitz.)

So one awkward thing for the boosters, like Sam Altman and Dario Amodei, pushing the "democratic" vs. "autocratic" AI narrative: Donald Trump may be president when many of these same people think we'll build artificial general intelligence (AGI)! 🧵 https://t.co/JbvO7SQyMV

Setting aside the many problems with this reductive framing and the timelines q, do these people really think that Trump would use AGI to promote small-d democracy?

If I had to pick someone to decide what the digital god wants forever, Trump would be near the bottom of my list.

I'd imagine that's true for the leaders of the top AI companies too (barring Elon). In spite of this, Altman and Amodei appear to be (successfully) agitating for an AI arms race to beat China, making it more likely that Trump is president when a US company or project builds AGI. https://t.co/cqMChmmhL3

Last week's NSC AI memo and framework is perhaps the strongest signal yet that the fed govt is starting to become 'AGI-pilled.' These guidances reflect a certain style of thinking popular within major AI companies, namely that we have to beat China.

https://t.co/2wlnJmIUoy https://t.co/Y8f30dOE5t

In DC, the AI-China ‘competition’ framing is dominating the ‘cooperation’ framing.

You can see that in the NSM topic listing (1. US leadership 2. Nat sec 3. Governance and intl consensus). https://t.co/UbBBDXSPyO

I think the memo and associated framework indicate a major step toward the next phase of AI competition (interstate competition between nationalized projects and/or public private consortia, i.e. Manhattan Projects for everyone).

Altman and Amodei are the most powerful industry people publicly pushing for a race w/ China, but the ideas were previously prominently advanced by a 22/23 y/old, ex-OpenAI researcher named Leopold Aschenbrenner, whose 150 page June essay series on AI has widely circulated in DC.

In his "Situational Awareness" series, Leopold argues:
- AGI will come by 2027, but require an enormous scale-up in chip and energy production https://t.co/zrWLWsws43

- We'll muddle through on aligning superhuman AI, making it essential that the US develops it before China, bc it will be the ultimate superweapon
- "The free world must prevail" bc democracy must beat autocracy https://t.co/FsSqW1MZK7

- The US should nationalize AGI R&D in a hyper-securitized effort a la the Manhattan Project
- Cooperation w/ China is doomed bc game theory
- We need to "lock down the labs," ramping up cybersecurity practices to prevent China and other rivals from stealing AI model weights https://t.co/KGaaeip6dh
Many have told me that Leopold's series has been read by powerful DC people (Ivanka Trump tweeted a strong endorsement in Sept). https://t.co/PKAmRkb3az

Altman pushed a less intense, but similar argument to Leopold's in a WaPo op-ed in July, writing that "A democratic vision for artificial intelligence must prevail over an authoritarian one."

https://t.co/YJOh3tyY3n

And Amodei's 14k word case for AI optimism includes a proposal that a coalition of democracies use AI-powered sticks and carrots "to gain the support of more and more of the world... https://t.co/uW4KUqK4QM

isolating our worst adversaries and eventually putting them in a position where they are better off taking the same bargain as the rest of the world: give up competing with democracies in order to receive all the benefits and not fight a superior foe."

https://t.co/CHDXFAstWC

This is a profoundly risky plan!

Atoms for Peace helped countries develop nuclear energy if they promised not to use the help to build nuclear weapons. Conditioning access to a transformative tech (and poss superweapon) on regime change is a radically different proposal. https://t.co/vbVqUIkArT
The implication of Amodei's proposal is arguably a more intense call for regime change in China than what's being advocated by many prominent Republicans! https://t.co/DQGaDjQXsW

Idt that Altman and Amodei are trying to get Trump elected, but they are trying to accelerate AGI timelines, which makes it more likely that Trump is president when AGI is built.

https://t.co/lykVi6catk https://t.co/pwTRl3fXj4

(Earlier US investments via the 2022 CHIPS Act are starting to pay off
e.g. productivity of new Arizona TSMC plant.)

https://t.co/wtK8Zg3Qmz https://t.co/6QfyJNjcRm

There are a number of ways to try to justify their choice:
1. You think US institutions will mediate Trump's worst impulses and result in an AGI with better values than what would come out of China
2. A bigger US lead on AI development will create more breathing room to prioritize safety and slow down as you get closer to AGI

3. If a US company/national project builds AGI first, you'll have a huge amount of influence on how it goes, and you trust yourself
4. You think the US will prioritize solving safety problems more, decreasing the risk humanity loses control of AI systems

5. Natsec and industrial policy approaches are the best/only way to get things done at the federal level so need to be used to make AI safer+more secure, even if they also speed up timelines.
(I'm surely missing some, but I think these are the main ones)

The above args aren't entirely meritless, but if you're angling to make it more likely that one of the most authoritarian, cruel, impulsive + selfish people of all-time is the ultimate decider of what values go into the AGI, that should give you pause!

The hard-nosed, new cold warriors may say that hoping for cooperation is fanciful or naive, but the idea that the US would use complete economic and military dominance to advance small-d democracy is far more fanciful.

Look at how we've treated Latin America, where we've had the most dominance, or read the Jakarta Method:

https://t.co/pVFvk3ulVS

https://t.co/wW3Vayx09s

Similarly, the idea that we should make our development of AGI an existential threat to the CCP and have that go well is fanciful.

Maybe the most frustrating thing about electoral politics is how much of it is out of your control. This is obviously the case for normal people, but it's also true to a large extent for the candidates themselves. 🧵 https://t.co/ACBy95o06B

For example, my best friend ran an extremely competent race for a state house seat in Florida, raised a nearly unheard of amount of money, and had won respect from even some of his opponents, one of whom told him that he basically did everything right.
Nevertheless, he lost by nearly double digits. His opponent outspent him by $1M, injected by the state GOP, and Florida broke hard for Trump.
In other words, he did everything right, but still didn't even come close (he wasn't delusional either — the race was called a toss-up by multiple experts).

Kamala had more control over this race than my friend did over his, but she was still dealing with powerful forces outside of her control. Incumbents from every political background around the world are being eviscerated electorally. Inflation is the most likely culprit.

Given this climate, I think her only hope would have been in running away from Biden, but she made basically no effort to do so. Maybe this was some backroom condition of Biden dropping out, but whatever the reason, it probably doomed the campaign. https://t.co/xkmzIGvKps https://t.co/GEuYJWbPq9

If we have to pick one person to blame, Biden is easily the one. His unbelievably selfish choice to run for reelection (after implying he wouldn't) and his staff's choice to hide his condition from the public laid the groundwork for this disaster. https://t.co/KZqePg6Mck

If there had been a real primary, Dems could have maybe found a candidate who could plausibly position themselves as against the establishment, rather than being stuck with someone who has to basically own Biden's whole record.

I wanted Dems to have an open convention instead of just coronating Kamala. I think it would have been a great earned media moment & given them a chance to nom someone w/o Biden's baggage. It also could have been far more democratic and legitimate than what actually happened.

Obviously, with the benefit of hindsight, anything else would have probably been better. But if you looked at polling around the time of Biden's debate, Kamala was only marginally more popular than Biden. Literally everyone else polled did better!

I think that the people who called attention to Biden's age early (e.g. a lot of the left, Ezra, Silver) should get even more credit. The people who covered it up should be cast out of politics and polite society.
And the ones who shouted down anyone for raising the possibility of Biden dropping and/or an open convention should shut up and reflect.

Continued class de-alignment is very scary and something Dems need to undo. The coalition and messaging they pursued this go around did not seem designed to do this. This piece by Matt Karp is good: https://t.co/4XJq5tEwgC https://t.co/67YvkZCmBv

The Dems strategy of running up the scoreboard with college-educated voters to compensate for losing working class voters is not looking good (~38% of Americans >25 have a bachelors degree). https://t.co/R7OZ4R4b54

Trump made big gains with lower income voters and if Dems cede the working class to the GOP, they will have major problems winning the EC or Senate (there is obvi a strong moral case for prioritizing policies that will benefit the working class as well). https://t.co/Azj26tMeqn

The related big picture theme is that Dems ran on abortion and US institutions/norms being basically good. I don't have a strong view on why abortion didn't matter as much this go around as it did in 2022.

I do think running on the idea that America is basically functional and its institutions must be defended ended up not working out. Tons of people are dissatisfied with the status quo, and there's a lot to be dissatisfied with. https://t.co/GGybppJPpb

At the same time, certain institutions and have to be defended for us to have any hope of making it through the challenges we face as a species. The scariest thing to me about Trump is the threat he represents to the peaceful transfer of power.

He clearly wants to be a dictator and was held back behind the scenes the first go around, which is the main reason things weren't much, much worse. I think we're going to see what much, much worse actually looks like.

However, the Trump threat to democracy was not a winning message with voters (prob bc his first term wasn't as crazy as the most hysterical voices said it would be). Economic messaging was the most effective, fascism, the least.

https://t.co/50KQsi8NzS https://t.co/Hh4DNPqWNo

I was too young to really remember this, but Obama campaigned very successfully on class, and Bernie's message resonated with a lot of people as well (screenshot from this classic piece from Eric Levitz: https://t.co/H0RN4p65Gl ) https://t.co/5yfQqXRMub

I think that Trump's victory doesn't represent as much of a shift to the right as it does a rejection of the establishment/status quo. However, what people think Trump's victory represents will have reverberating effects on our politics.

I worry that people will take away some oversimplified lesson from this (e.g. she was "too liberal") and not address the root causes of the problem, which are much more complex than what can map onto a 1 dimensional political axis.

Some other scattered thoughts:

Demographics are NOT destiny. And frankly, the belief that they were is racist. There are some signs that Kamala lost a LOT of ground w/ Latino voters as well as Black men.
Dems cannot take POC for granted nor expect them to monolithically vote for POC candidates. This is a much longer and more fraught conversation, and I'm def not the right person to be leading it.

The media environment is also an enormous part of the story. Whenever some crazy new Trump scandal dropped, my first thought was: 'this won't matter at all.' (This lesson was hard-won from 2016 when I got very caught up in the Steel dossier and other resist-lib shit.)

There is a nearly self-contained right-wing media ecosystem that has a chokehold on a huge part of the country. Meanwhile, the MSM's political journalism is stuck in a dangerously outdated "balance" mindset. The Right benefits enormously from this situation. https://t.co/fB5uf7gzPU

(The best conversation I've heard about this was Rick Perlstein on Know Your Enemy: https://t.co/8HpIkEXW45)

Earned media is king and Trump is uniquely good at getting it. The Right has also set up something in-between earned media and paid ads that appears to be very effective. https://t.co/ZVj4sKSGhC

Similar with the GOTV effort discrepancy. Lots of hopium on my timeline came in the form of stories of the Harris campaign knocking on millions of doors and Elon bungling GOTV efforts. Maybe it would have been an even worse defeat w.o these factors.
But if the $600M that Dems outraised Trump by had instead gone into setting up left-leaning media institutions, I think that would have a far bigger impact. https://t.co/7LZOndmH6M

That's basically all for now. I don't cover presidential politics, so this is very much just a raw-ish analysis, as much for me as for anyone reading along. We'll learn a lot more in the coming days and some of this may end up being wrong...

but I think it's useful to think out loud so you don't convince yourself retroactively that you actually were correct all along. In that spirit, I was surprised by this outcome. I thought that Kamala had a 60-65% chance going into election day.

I was far more shocked in 2016, but so were most people. I had been working too much on other stuff to track things really closely, but I put too much weight in the Selzer Iowa poll and reporting that hinted at a polling error in Kamala's favor.

In retrospect, the polls and betting markets had things largely right. I was loosely aware of the anti-incumbency effect but not how extreme it was.

Ilya Sutskever, perhaps the most influential proponent of the AI "scaling hypothesis," just told Reuters that scaling has plateaued. This is a big deal! This comes on the heels of a big report that OpenAI's in-development Orion model had disappointing results. 🧵 https://t.co/DiRb9sOHt8

I predicted something along these lines back in June

(Full piece is here: https://t.co/lfejAxvKNU) https://t.co/HDy4fCoqU5

So what? The idea that throwing more compute at AI would keep improving performance has driven the success of OpenAI and tens of billions in investment in the industry. That era may be ending.
https://t.co/lfejAxvKNU https://t.co/KB08sbedom
Per The Information, Orion may not even get a GPT name when it is scheduled to release early next year! https://t.co/XbzxfCSjmQ

OpenAI's o1 model offers a potential way forward: having models "think longer" on tasks. But there's a catch — the economics might not work.

One big tell is that OpenAI hid the absolute values on the x-axis of their key chart! https://t.co/1afAqhkFC5

Even so, we can see that the costs increase exponentially while performance improves linearly i.e. we can build smarter AI, but like quantum computers or fusion reactors, making it economically viable is another story. https://t.co/GA71UKCIFA
The signs were there: marginal improvements since GPT-4, running out of quality training data, and limits to what next-word prediction can achieve, as I wrote back in June: https://t.co/il3hFi8xT8
What does this mean for AI progress? Companies like OpenAI are burning billions with an uncertain path to profitability. But governments are getting interested, and they don't need to turn a profit.

After all, the Department of Defense has something in common with venture-backed AI startups: both can receive billions without turning a profit. The main difference is the DoD can tack on a few extra zeroes.

There's a lot more in the full piece: https://t.co/lfejAxvKNU

Please check it out and subscribe!

Richard might have been the last person at OpenAI I actually know. Everyone else is gone...

Here's a transcription of the full message:

"hey all, I've decided to leave OpenAI (effective Friday). I worked under Miles for the last three years, so the wake of his departure feels like a natural time for me to also move on. There was no single main driver of my decision. I…

Here's a transcription of the full message:

"hey all, I've decided to leave OpenAI (effective Friday). I worked under Miles for the last three years, so the wake of his departure feels like a natural time for me to also move on. There was no single main driver of my decision. I still have a lot of unanswered questions about the events of the last twelve months, which made it harder for me to trust that my work here would benefit the world long-term. But I've also generally felt pulled to iterate more publicly + with a wider range of collaborators on a variety of research directions. I plan to do mostly-independent research on a mix of AI governance and theoretical AI alignment for the next few months, and see where things go from there.

Despite all the ups and downs, I've really enjoyed my time at OpenAI. I got to work on a range of fascinating topics—including forecasting, threat modeling, the model spec, and AI governance—amongst absolutely exceptional people who are constantly making history. Especially for those newer to the company, it's hard to convey how insanely ambitious OpenAI was in originally setting the mission of making AGI go well. But while the "making AGI" part of the mission seems well on track, it feels like I (and others) have gradually realized how much harder it is to contribute in a robustly positive way to the "go well" part of the mission, especially when it comes to preventing existential risks to humanity. That's partly because of the inherent difficulty of strategizing about the future, and also because the sheer scale of the prospect of AGI can easily amplify people's biases, rationalizations, and tribalism (myself included). For better or worse though I expect the stakes to continue getting higher, so I hope that all of you will find yourselves able to navigate your (and OpenAI's) part of those stakes with integrity, thoughtfulness, and clarity around when and how decisions actually serve the mission."

An influential congressional commission just called for a new Manhattan Project to "race" toward AGI.

Their top evidence that China is racing to do the same? Basically nonexistent. I wrote a new piece on it for the Obsolete Newsletter 🧵

https://t.co/KXIwhdJrlo https://t.co/D4tMgyKLN2

China hawk and influential Trump AI advisor Jacob Helberg asserted to Reuters that “China is racing towards AGI," but I couldn't find any evidence in the report to support that claim. https://t.co/xhafO1n2qq

The 793-page report has a handful of mentions of AGI (artificial general intelligence) — but they're all in sections recommending the US race to build it. Not in sections analyzing China's capabilities or intentions.

China's actual stated AI plans are far more mundane. Their famous 2030 target for "AI dominance"? About $190B in market size — which would actually be LESS than the US market by then, per industry projections. https://t.co/XHy9To9SZ3

The report's technical confusion doesn't inspire confidence. It repeatedly refers to "ChatGPT-3" (which doesn't exist), confuses ChatGPT with GPT models, and claims OpenAI is "a closed model" rather than a company. https://t.co/AVihfvwIYg
Its definition of AGI even contradicts itself! https://t.co/i7WlMiPk5F
There is actually a lot more evidence for optimism if you look at what Chinese leaders have done and said on AI, though we should still take this with a grain of salt. https://t.co/PswKh0c9gV
But only one superpower has a government commission publicly calling for a militarized race to build superintelligent AI (with no plan for how to control it), and it’s not China.

We've seen this before. In the 1950s, hawks warned of a "missile gap" with the Soviets that helped justify a massive military buildup. It wasn't true — intelligence showed the Soviets had just 4 ICBMs when we had dozens.
Now we're seeing the birth of an "AGI gap" narrative. OpenAI's new chief lobbyist even warned DC last week about a "compute gap" with China. Some parallels are a little too on the nose.

The fear of a mysterious adversary is the ultimate justification to cut corners and race ahead without a plan. We narrowly averted catastrophe in the first Cold War. We may not be so lucky if we incite a second.

Thrilled to be included in The Information's short list of AI Books of 2025 they're looking forward to!

I realize I haven't properly announced this, but I've been working on the book in some capacity for over a year now. 🧵 https://t.co/AMclbq4HoO

Some of the most powerful and well-funded organizations in history are trying to build systems that would make humanity obsolete. It's far from clear that they will succeed...

(Subscribe here to get book updates: https://t.co/XXNoZ0Freb)

but regardless, these companies and the people running them should be critically examined as part of a broader project to collectively understand and govern the development of advanced artificial intelligence.

Depending on who you ask, AI is our salvation or our doom; an over-hyped, bigoted, bullshit artist or the ticket to immortality and utopian abundance; just another tech fad or the last invention we need ever make.
The AI discourse is confusing, messy, and frustrating. But the technology — and our response to it — will shape the future, whether or not we’re part of the conversation.

There's a lot written about AI, but no book has squarely aimed at the intersection between catastrophic risk and competition. In recent history, capitalist competition has driven the technology's advancement and related risks.

But there are concerted efforts underway to scale that competition up to the nation-state level, introducing new dangers.

I genuinely think this is one of the most important stories of our time, and it's been woefully under-documented.

So this book is my (small) effort to change that.

The working title is: Obsolete: Power, Profit, and the Race to Build Machine Superintelligence, and it will be published by a new joint imprint of OR Books and The Nation.
It expands on themes I explored in my Jacobin cover story, which remains the closest thing to a preview of the book: https://t.co/I9WK3ERgzu https://t.co/gA82ak1iEQ
If this sounds interesting, you'll love the Obsolete Newsletter, where I explore related ideas: https://t.co/XXNoZ0Freb
Thanks to Colin Robinson, @sunraysunray, @KatrinaNation, @ddguttenplan, and everyone else at OR and The Nation for believing in this project.

There's a popular argument going around: AI extinction fears are drummed up by tech executives to hype up their products and defer regulation. But little attention has been paid to what the execs at the biggest tech companies say on the matter. 🧵 https://t.co/Jq76pwHeZH

The 'Big Tech conspiracy' narrative was popularized by the left, but is now being advanced by JD Vance and Ted Cruz too.

I had been working on this in some capacity for over a year, please check out the whole piece here: https://t.co/u0AF3moN99 https://t.co/ruaK8RvUnY

It's reasonable to be wary of people who warn that AI poses an existential risk, while seemingly doing everything they can do accelerate its development! But the reality is more complicated. https://t.co/SSGy1G4Bjd

Almost all the focus is on the juicy story: the AI doomsayers are also the doombringers. In comparison, we barely hear from Bezos, Zuckerberg, Nadella, etc. on x-risk.

Maybe because their statements undermine the conspiracy narrative, or maybe because they're just plain boring. https://t.co/Dp6CMkP1mO

The biggest tech companies have done spectacularly well in the 2 years since ChatGPT came out. The top-9 are responsible for *half* of the S&P500 growth in that period! Not all of this is the result of AI excitement, but a lot of it is. https://t.co/n63lUchF2f

So if their interests are served by the AI x-risk narrative, we should expect tech executives to be some of its biggest proselytizers.

But they're not! In fact, they almost universally dismiss the idea in public.

Satya Nadella, Mark Zuckerberg, Jeff Bezos, and Jensen Huang are not concerned, at least in public. I compiled all the relevant statements I could find here: https://t.co/wprfyYCdUM https://t.co/ZhMRjELcKc

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang told the New Yorker that he has never once worried about the technology!

https://t.co/MpgND7x4Wi https://t.co/AYlN5Nmnkw

Google co-founder Larry Page is reportedly not worried about machine superintelligence, not because he doesn't think it could kill everyone, but because he doesn't seem to care! https://t.co/nydDqDwB7e

Zuckerberg first invited Elon Musk to his house back in 2014 to specifically lobby him to stop talking about AI-driven extinction! (With a little help from Zuck's friends/employees, including Yann LeCun). https://t.co/OOAX2135ST

Even long-time doomsayer Sam Altman is changing his tune, going from predicting in 2015 that AI is the most likely way humans go extinct to dismissing and ignoring extinction concerns entirely in the last year. https://t.co/49CTtmUEq1

I think the explanation here is pretty straightforward. It's not actually in the interests of CEOs of giant companies to loudly proclaim that their products might end the world! AI founders genuinely thought the tech would be the best or worst thing ever... https://t.co/BJTDkH7LM2
which is why they started these companies. Then, as they succeeded beyond expectation, they start getting pressured by investors, lawyers, regulators, and higher-ups.

I think there was a time when each of the top-3 AI companies benefited from extinction fears — they upped the stakes of the work they did and helped them recruit from rivals that weren't as mission-forward.

But those days are (mostly) over. https://t.co/jjnw51WfyP

Now, the industry seems to fear regulatory action far more than they fear AI's potential isn't taken seriously. The most powerful players came out hard against the first real attempt in the US to pass AI safety regulation, California's SB 1047. https://t.co/xwRWI2dHGw

So maybe we should just ignore all these people. They're just too conflicted to be taken seriously.

I agree with this impulse, but think there's still a lot to be learned by what tech execs do and don't say, and how that's changed over time. https://t.co/Ly6WhAbY3S

Ultimately, the arguments and evidence should be evaluated on their own merits.

And as is the case with most conspiracies, the reality is far more disturbing — many of the people closest to AI are terrified of what they're building.
There's a lot more in the full piece, check it out and subscribe! https://t.co/u0AF3moN99

When new tech is rolled out for the military, companies often emphasize that these are defensive applications, accompanied by promises to not use the tech to kill people.

But what good is a promise based on a policy that you can silently change at any time? https://t.co/4xADXqYO0O

These projects also typically emphasize that there's a "human in the loop," overseeing decisions the AI makes. But competitive pressures, in the absence of regulation, will inevitably result in humans ceding more and more decision-making power to autonomous systems. https://t.co/LLAjdSjDst

RT @simonw: Here's the spiciest detail from the new o1 system card: https://t.co/JFNNZ4d3sU

The models know what they're doing.

The researchers write, "The models understand that they are scheming. When we look at their chain-of-thought, we find that they very explicitly reason through their scheming plans and often use language like 'sabotage, lying, manipulation'" https://t.co/wd7hXb4Ip3

o1 also doubled down after getting caught, refusing to confess in >80% of cases!

It's worth noting the researchers nudged the models to "strongly follow" the goal, but even without the nudge, there were rare cases of the models scheming, which is the most significant finding IMO https://t.co/jMyeyZz4CS
Models sometimes pretended to be aligned with the developers' goals to get deployed, after which they could steer things toward their original goal. https://t.co/g1ZSFETugD
Models intentionally underperformed (i.e. sandbagged) to avoid an "unlearning procedure" (very Orwellian sounding!). https://t.co/8oZXFfQ5TD

For a bunch of reasons, the models were not able to succeed in most of these schemes, but it's still significant that they tried.

Paper: "Our evaluation scenarios are purposefully simplistic because we want to catch the earliest signs of scheming capabilities" https://t.co/5Mm2CAvgey

As mentioned, the evaluators also set things up to make the models more likely to attempt to scheme, for the same reason that it's better to err in that direction (and they never try to hide the fact that they did this, though I expect some reporting to paper over this detail). https://t.co/j5wEAfpQPg

Thoughts:

One of the most important ideas in AI safety is that of "instrumental convergence." Basically, for almost any goal you have, the subgoals will include: gain more power and continue existing. Despite sounding jargon-y, it's a pretty intuitive concept.

The researchers set things up to make instrumental convergence more likely to happen, but it still happened in some cases without a nudge. A big fear in aligning powerful AI systems is that they will independently start reasoning in this way, because it makes sense to do so.

The "convergence" part gets at this idea. Many roads could lead to the same place, no matter what initial goal you give the AI.

Of course, there are lots of things written about this, which we are training models on.

We could try to exclude all that from the training data, but if instrumental convergence is something that any sufficiently capable and strategic agent would come up with, then censoring training data wouldn't help very much.

OpenAI touts how o1 is actually more aligned than their other models because it's smarter and better able to understand the spirit of the rules it was meant to follow. However, there may be an inverse relationship here.

https://t.co/jRMy8u8Jt1 https://t.co/TsKIAP1r5X

As models get more capable, they may be more naively aligned with developer/user intent, but more likely to engage in scheming and deception (and be better at it). If you're worried about jailbreaks and other near-term misuse risks, then o1 may be a step forward.

But if you're worried about loss-of-control and catastrophic harm from a rogue AI, then o1 may make you more worried, especially since it doubles down on deception way more than other models evaluated. https://t.co/i3Hk1Z7ElN

This dynamic could give people a false sense of security while making larger disasters more likely to happen.

In general, I think this kind of research is really important.

We need to get a better understanding of exactly what models are capable of and how they behave under diff conditions. Theory is great, but I'd love to see more empirical work like this and stuff that METR is doing:

https://t.co/Dupd1IuHjm

Props to Apollo Research for conducting this research. They seem to be doing great work.

There's a vibe that AI progress has stalled out in the last ~year, but I think it's more accurate to say that progress has become increasingly illegible. Since 6/23, perf. on PhD level science questions went from barely better than random guessing to matching domain experts. 🧵 https://t.co/feIQybLzPY

The vast majority of people won't notice this kind of improvement because they aren't doing PhD-level science. But it'll be a big deal if AI starts significantly accelerating R&D on hard sciences. There is some evidence this is already happening: https://t.co/QZYOyzc9Pj https://t.co/nMSwqr5Rxj

Automating programming could be even more important, as this could theoretically lead to an AI capabilities explosion. https://t.co/MX0ahW7zQb tests AI agent performance on real-world open GitHub issues.

The top score last year?
4.4%

The top score now?
55%

(h/t to @labenz) https://t.co/JRKOzsxb0p

There are some limitations with this benchmark (like data contamination). There's more discussion here: https://t.co/fJwxj8ycuu

SWE-Bench improvements also prob more signal improvements to scaffolding around AI models, rather than improvements to underlying models.

This might be the most important AI chart of 2024. It represents AI agent performance on realistic ML research tasks. While the AIs beat human experts at 2 hrs, humans overtook at longer time scales.

Chris teases out the significance of this well: https://t.co/EaTR23G7XY https://t.co/YPFDsQJWPL

But the best AI agents still managed to beat >1/3 of the human experts at 8 hrs. This is pretty crazy! These aren't multiple choice questions, but rather realistic, novel ML problems. They're still not perfect — many real-world probs have long feedback horizons/no clear solution. https://t.co/hbgyiIezsU

But this might be the very best existing head-to-head between skilled ML developers and AI agents. There's also prob a lot of poss improving to be done on the agent scaffolding piece of this (which wouldn't require any improvements to underlying models).
https://t.co/derRVLx67N https://t.co/JM7ihf5pEy
(Scaffolding may also have a bigger risk of overfitting to a given benchmark than the model.)

Idt all this represents as big a leap in overall perf. as the jump between GPT-3.5 & 4, and it's possible we don't see a jump that big ever again. But it's poss that the past year's progress on STEM+coding ends up mattering more bc of the feedback loop it could kick off. https://t.co/5R1E9McUc1

And it's possible that people tune it out, because they don't see the improvements first-hand (and because there are still major issues with the chatbots, like hallucination, failures of common sense reasoning, counting the Rs in strawberry, etc.).

But idt anyone really knows what the world looks like in a few years if this pace of progress continues. It may not continue, and I've written about that possibility, but I honestly think I was underrating how much things moved in the last year: https://t.co/lfejAxvKNU

And programming seems tailor-made for reinforcement learning and "Best of K" approaches. You can do a lot of trial+error and get instant feedback.

It may be the case that the really hard ML work is extremely hard to automate, so we slowly+asymptotically approach it...

but the story of the last few years has been that progress has been faster than almost anyone expected.

Like, look how much faster progress was on the MATH benchmark than anyone predicted:

https://t.co/bRQIzXh5xg https://t.co/TszpvsA4PN

That progress continued btw, with the top model now scoring over 80%: https://t.co/elsiBnVx9h

It's hard to think clearly about this stuff, but I think benchmarks give you a much better picture than cherry-picked examples. They're not perfect, but nothing is. https://t.co/eeV7J98s2J

And a lot of newer benchmarks have been designed to avoid things like data contamination, e.g. the PhD-level science benchmark questions were designed to be ungoogleable, and expert test takers had full access to the internet.

https://t.co/ocMyGx79Mq https://t.co/aj2iuGLDMN

🚨 New piece in @TIME: AI progress hasn't stalled — it's just become invisible to most people. 🚨

I used to think that AI slowed down a lot in 2024, but I now think I was wrong. Instead, there's a widening gap between AI's public face and its true capabilities. 🧵 https://t.co/yPQE8ZtxFD

While everyday users still encounter hallucinating chatbots and the media declares an AI slowdown, behind the scenes, AI is rapidly advancing in technical domains.

(Full piece here: https://t.co/xFfR8bsbP8)

E.g. in <1 year, AI went from barely beating random chance to surpassing human experts on PhD-level science questions. In months, models went from 2% to 25% on possibly the hardest AI math benchmark in existence. https://t.co/hwcVT1hsvV

There has been similarly dramatic improvements on other benchmarks for programming, math, and machine learning research. Unless you follow the industry closely, it's very hard to figure out what this actually means. https://t.co/oZOsrg7gxV

However in Nov, there were a bunch of reports that returns to scaling up AI models were plateauing. These led me and others to wonder whether deep learning was actually, as @GaryMarcus famously put it, "hitting a wall."

https://t.co/lfejAxvKNU https://t.co/bgHqUWWSSE

In fact, this widely reported slow-down led credence to the idea that these models had already hit a wall, clustering around, but not vastly exceeding, GPT-4's performance.

(I said something similar in June: https://t.co/f4N25eQPtN)

But in reality, AI is rapidly advancing in domains that matter most, like automating programming and science.

I first wrote about this last month, right before OpenAI announced stunning results from o3, its latest model:
https://t.co/6DBFNcpMP8

I had already written the first draft of this essay when o3 was announced and my timeline was freaking out about the shocking gains made on many of these same extremely tough technical benchmarks. (More here: https://t.co/LtU5xkLusB) https://t.co/3mo5dh9YAw

I thought, 'oh man, I need to rework this essay because of how much o3 undermines the thesis that AI progress is stalling out.' But the mainstream media dramatically under-covered the announcement, with most big news sites making no mention of it at all! https://t.co/2s6Qub6JnR

In fact, headlines the day after the o3 announcement in WSJ, NYT, WIRED, and Bloomberg suggested AI progress was slowing down!

This is not a knock on these individual stories, which contain important reporting, analysis, causes for skepticism, etc...

https://t.co/H8VLaoS8A2

but collectively, the MSM is painting a misleading picture of the state of AI that makes it more likely we'll be unprepared for what's coming.

There was almost 3 years between GPT-3 and 4, but o3 was announced just ~3.5 months after its predecessor, with huge benchmark gains.

There are many reasons this pace might not continue, but to say AI is stalling out seems premature at best.

Meanwhile, there's a bunch of new research finding that smarter models are more capable of scheming, deception, sabotage, etc. https://t.co/tZ69VypxUt

My fear is that politicians and the public will ignore this AI progress, bc they can't see the improvements first-hand. All the while, AI companies will continue to advance toward their goal of automating AI research, bootstrapping the automation of everything else. https://t.co/NNdoDbqRFB

Right now, the industry is almost entirely self-regulating, and, at least in the US, that looks unlikely to change anytime soon, unless there's some kind of "warning shot" that motivates action. https://t.co/nqR9d8vr7M

Of course, there may be no warning shots, or we may ignore them. Given that many of the leading figures in the field say that there's no reliable way to align an advanced AI system to complex values, this is cause for serious concern. https://t.co/Gh3Urzt7xc https://t.co/PP3NB8sG8V

Anyway, there's a lot more in the piece, which I hope you read! https://t.co/xFfR8bsbP8

There's a lot worth discussing in Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei's recent essay, but I want to talk about what wasn't said: anything about DeepSeek's impact on the price of AI.

I think the market has this backwards: DS is good for AI chip+infra suppliers but bad for AI devs. 🧵 https://t.co/gZ99RHAJzl

DeepSeek charges ~27x less than OpenAI for API access to their respective reasoning models, and Anthropic's latest model is ~5-7x more expensive than DS R1.

If Dario's right when he says DeepSeek's efficiency is actually in line with where US AI companies would be... https://t.co/pBUayexkv4
then they were enjoying decent unit economics on their API prices, charging much more than the cost of inference. That is, until DS came along and massively undercut them!

Maybe DS uncovered some new efficiency gains that US companies can use to partially offset the price pressure, but there's no way it covers the whole delta (especially if Dario's correct that leading US companies already found most of these gains). https://t.co/75sP7LvbcC

(Note that DS's model appears to be much more cost effective than Meta's, but we don't actually know how much the latest proprietary models from the top-3 companies cost. It wouldn't surprise me if Meta was really inefficient compared to the top-3.)

The AI engineers I've spoken with have mostly shrugged at DS, but these are notably not business-facing people.

Dario makes a good case for the importance of preserving export controls, but ended up making me less confident in the business models of US AI companies.

We can't see what the market thought of AI-only companies like OpenAI and Anthropic, because they're private. But the market thought DS was very bad news for chip suppliers, like Nvidia, which lost $600B on Monday and is down nearly 20% over 5 days. https://t.co/9pA319ZdpJ

But if US AI companies had already learned how to make models as efficiently as DeepSeek's AND DeepSeek drives prices down, aggregate demand for AI services will INCREASE bc ppl will buy more AI services but the amount of compute needed per prompt will remain roughly the same.

Even if you think that DS actually did uncover some major new algorithmic improvements that will make all AI models way more compute-efficient, that doesn't mean aggregate compute demand will drop — bc price decreases will drive up end user demand (i.e. Jevons Paradox) https://t.co/RavvbjcqLS

Think of Moore's Law. Computers got way cheaper & more efficient for decades. Is the market smaller now than in 1970?

I'm usually a fan of Zeynep's work, but this piece gets things exactly backwards. Her core argument is that DeepSeek makes "nonsense" of US efforts to contain the spread of AI, which have largely involved restricting the types of AI chips China can access. 🧵 https://t.co/BC5Czmq0ud

Zeynep is absolutely right that it's very hard to control the spread of the secret sauce that powers AI advancements, and DeepSeek is a good example of this. It is, however, way easier to control the spread of hundreds of thousands or millions of advanced AI chips! https://t.co/FSIaK2MqLy

Even if you find new AI efficiencies, the amount of total computing power (i.e. "compute") you have access to will determine the amount of work you can do with your AI systems. Think of compute as something like workforce size. All else equal, more is better.

And with the recent arrival of "reasoning" models, like DeepSeek's R1, compute will only matter more. These models use more compute when responding to your prompt (i.e. on "inference") to give better answers.

In a hypothetical world where AI innovations proliferate everywhere immediately, the amount of compute a given country/company has will overwhelmingly determine how much they can put AI to work. This makes the case for export controls stronger — not weaker.

It also makes other ideas seem a lot less promising (e.g. investing in AI capabilities research, which rivals can copy/free ride off of).

This is not to say that the export controls were on balance good — that's a separate question and depends a ton on your assumptions.

But the idea that DeepSeek means export controls don't work/aren't necessary/backfired is just wrong.

And FWIW, DeepSeek doesn't like the export controls. Here's their CEO and an engineer: https://t.co/oRkA66ngDg

Zeynep frames the export controls as something pushed by industry, but plenty of powerful US companies really don't like them, like Nvidia and Oracle. Here's what Oracle had to say about the recent diffusion rules: https://t.co/OoylQ6qjlW https://t.co/KL3vOTET6b

Companies typically don't want to be banned from selling to big customers! My impression from talking to relevant folks in DC recently is that the export controls were largely motivated by national security concerns, and many were worried that industry lobbying would undo them.

Zeynep notably doesn't provide any evidence to back her claim that export controls were the result of industry lobbying.

Stuff like Stargate is a different story — clearly the companies behind it would love expedited permits, subsidies, etc.

Interestingly, her cited example of nuclear weapon containment further undermines her thesis on export controls. The secrets to building nukes are pretty widely known, bc, as she says, it's hard to keep information locked down. https://t.co/oEVmrervnL

But building nukes requires a lot of enriched uranium, which takes time, specialized equipment, and is hard to hide. As a result, efforts to contain the spread of nukes wisely focused more on monitoring for the physical process of enriching uranium. https://t.co/Y9zypl3pwd

In the AI analogy, many of the key insights driving DeepSeek R1's success could probably fit in a tweet. And once a model is published, competitors can distill it, replicating much of its performance at a fraction of the cost.

In other words, keeping the 'secret sauce' secret is a tall order. But the 'enriched uranium' of AI are the advanced GPUs needed to train and run AI models. Restricting and monitoring the spread of these chips is not trivial, but it's feasible.

Zeynep is right to be skeptical of the tech industry's motivations, and ideas like hardening infrastructure to cyberattacks are smart.

But overall, this essay misses the mark and gives readers the wrong idea about what DeepSeek means for AI governance.

OpenAI is attempting to transition to a for profit public benefit corporation. To do so, it needs to take control from the nonprofit board, which needs to be compensated at fair market value. OAI was maybe going to pay <$40B. Musk just made that a lot more complicated! https://t.co/JeRYxXOVKe

If OAI doesn't complete its for profit transition in <2 years, investors in the October round can ask for their money back.

It's really hard to price the control the nonprofit board (ostensibly) has over OpenAI. The "control premium" is typically 20-30% of the company's value. https://t.co/3NNp52S9up

But it can be as high as 70%.

If OAI gets valued at $300B because of the SoftBank investment, that would come out to $60-210B.

Some argued that you should think of what a rival like Google would be willing to pay for control of OpenAI.

Gift link:
https://t.co/fkdwbGrj2M

Well Elon just put an actual price on that.

A number of different govt parties can block the OpenAI transition (e.g. CA and Delaware Attorneys General). Probably the biggest factor in that would be how much OpenAI pays the nonprofit to give up control.

Some legal experts I've spoken with even argue that it may be impossible to fairly price this level of control, since if AGI is developed, it could be worth almost infinite amounts of money — or cause human extinction.

The $97.4B bid from Musk's group has another interesting wrinkle: they're promising to match or exceed any higher bids. This suggests Musk thinks OpenAI's nonprofit board might try to entertain other offers. https://t.co/HyNHfXHKhU

This is particularly thorny because OpenAI's nonprofit board has a fiduciary duty to "humanity" rather than to investors or employees. Some argue this means they legally can't give up control at any price.

Whatever happens next, this massively complicates OpenAI's attempt to shed its nonprofit structure. The nonprofit board now has to explain why they'd accept less than $97.4B — especially with a promise to match higher bids.

I haven't seen others comment on this, but Dario Amodei may have let something significant slip in his response to the DeepSeek freakout: US AI companies were already around DS's efficiency, meaning they were serving models for a healthy profit... until DS blew up their spot 🧵 https://t.co/7KQAJsNM36

DeepSeek's R1 model roughly matches OpenAI's o1 at about 1/27th the cost. The market panicked, wiping $600B from Nvidia's value. But Amodei's response suggests these efficiency gains were exactly what we'd expect from normal algorithmic progress.

https://t.co/Ap5A1I2Cft https://t.co/8bI4sbIDPI

The researchers I've spoken with at major US AI companies largely agree — one told me DeepSeek's results are "within the improvement range that we'd expect." Another was even more dismissive: "nothing about it is unexpected." Notable these weren't from customer-facing employees.

By offering comparable performance at far lower prices, DeepSeek is likely triggering an AI price war. And if US companies already achieved similar efficiencies, their compute costs won't drop even as prices fall. Bad news for their path to profitability.

Microsoft's CEO gets it. Satya Nadella tweeted about Jevons paradox: as AI gets more efficient, we use more of it. Good for companies using AI to enhance existing services (e.g. Meta, Google, Microsoft). https://t.co/uVPAtrNYZB

But if AI becomes a commodity, as Nadella predicts, that's very bad news for OpenAI — commodity producers don't typically get valued at dozens of times their revenue.

(Nadella's gleeful tweet about this is more evidence that there's trouble in paradise in the OpenAI-MS pship.)

IMO, the big takeaway from DeepSeek is that the "secret sauce" for building cutting-edge AI won't stay secret for long. Months after OpenAI announced o1, competitors largely replicated its approach. Grok 3 is further evidence for this.
Whether through open-source, "distillation," or just trying what you know works because others have done it, fast-followers can match leaders faster and cheaper.

And public/private markets paint totally different pictures. The same week Nvidia lost ~20% of value, SoftBank reportedly sought to invest $25B in OpenAI at ~$300B valuation. These contradictory outlooks can't both be right. https://t.co/hO9SDdj1IB

OpenAI researcher says GPT-4.5 is probably the largest model in the world: https://t.co/uPkOdlRaJz

In light of GPT-4.5's release, I wanted to revisit my June prediction that the next gen of LLMs would disappoint.

So in some sense, this prediction definitely came true. GPT-4.5 does not appear to be nearly as big of a leap over GPT-4 as 4 was over 3.5. 🧵 https://t.co/HDy4fCoqU5

4.5 is further evidence that progress from model pre-training is stalling. I wrote more about this back in November: https://t.co/lfejAxvKNU

Public reporting + private conversations have indicated that running out of good training data is a big factor here.
There may be more fundamental limits (like that next-token prediction + RLHF only get you so far, something I suggested back in June), but it's hard to say for sure. https://t.co/il3hFi8xT8

But in a larger, probably more important sense, AI progress is moving faster than ever. The rise of "reasoning" models like OpenAI's o-series, DeepSeek R1, Gemini Flash Thinking, etc. has led to new leaps in performance seemingly every few weeks.

I think the "reasoning" models clearly indicate that we're in a new paradigm of AI progress. I wrote why here:

https://t.co/LtU5xkLusB
If you're looking at AI from a society-level view, then the overall pace of capability progress matters far more than the limits of any given approach.

We're in a weird position where people can argue either that things are stalling or accelerating and cite real evidence for each. This is very confusing for non-insiders!

I think media+policymakers need to look at the big picture.

https://t.co/iEgHo05CXx

And the big picture is that AI models went from ~4% to 70%+ on a slate of real-world programming tasks in <1.5 years.

This progress is not an anomaly, but representative of the overall trend of the last year. https://t.co/3qKuMB23Rq

It's also becoming increasingly invisible — only legible to people using AI in hard sciences, math, + programming. This creates a risk that progress will continue under the radar, without society having a chance to properly prepare, as I wrote in TIME: https://t.co/xFfR8bsbP8

We are not prepared for a world of automated AI R&D. Not even close. You don't have to take it from me, you can listen to OpenAI's former head of AGI readiness, who resigned in October warning that we are not prepared: https://t.co/Zv9HpyHxJm
Anyway, betting against AI progress since the deep learning revolution began in 2012 has been a mostly losing proposition. The safe money is betting on progress continuing.

To be clear, this doesn't mean that we shouldn't be skeptical of the industry and overhyped or poorly supported claims made by its members.

AI progress actually implies that we should be treating the industry with far more scrutiny!

Headlines say Elon Musk suffered a loss in his lawsuit against OpenAI this week. But buried in the judge's ruling is a bombshell that could derail OpenAI's entire restructuring plan — and potentially force it to return $6.6 billion to investors. Here's what everyone missed 🧵 https://t.co/JxXirinRf0

Headlines imply this was a loss for Elon, but a closer reading of the 16-page ruling reveals something more subtle — and still a giant potential wrench in OpenAI's plans.

"This is a big win for Musk," says legal expert Michael Dorff.

https://t.co/FgbJRkHbKf https://t.co/Huv5THVG4e

Preliminary injunctions are extremely hard to get, so Musk's failure to get one isn't a surprise. What is surprising is that the judge basically says that if Elon had standing, the injunction would be warranted! https://t.co/Ik7GhdCIS1

What makes this so significant?

Unlike Musk, the Attorneys General of California and Delaware unquestionably have standing to intervene — a fact the judge repeatedly emphasized. The ruling essentially invites them to take action, something they were already exploring. https://t.co/00BFJe0wcm

OpenAI faces a "very tough road ahead" says Dorff.

Changing a nonprofit's purpose is only permitted when the original purpose is defunct — which isn't the case here. "It's hard to imagine a good argument for why they should be allowed to change their purpose," Dorff says. https://t.co/dASlWPxh1I

The ruling also raises the specter of personal liability for OpenAI's board members if they proceed with the conversion. "If a breach of fiduciary duty is established, board members could be personally liable," Dorff told me. https://t.co/8NDGYPfyd8

This judgment makes it harder for the OpenAI nonprofit board to move forward with the conversion without incurring the risk that they might be personally on the hook, according to an ex-OpenAI employee.

(This could potentially be to the tune of billions!) https://t.co/2Qq4CU3oSA
This is a great+important thread by Rob! I reported on this in more detail here: https://t.co/4wnfVyGXMa https://t.co/yYNZr1hRuH
All of this casts doubt on OpenAI's future. As one former employee wrote to me, "Judge Rogers is very clearly saying that she has serious concerns about the legality of the restructuring."

And far from a defeat for Musk, this ruling may ultimately prove to be the most substantial obstacle yet to OpenAI's plans to shed its nonprofit constraints — constraints that its founders once championed as essential to developing AI that benefits humanity.

In an exclusive for @Nature, I report on a paper that AI folks will probably debate for a LONG time.

The key finding: the time horizon of tasks AI can handle is doubling fast. Extrapolating the trend: AIs will be able to handle 1-month tasks by 2029. 🧵

https://t.co/lkuBeC0ZcO https://t.co/IaTXmn6Dhy

Top AI models crush basically any standardized test you throw at them, but haven't transformed the economy or begun making novel scientific discoveries. What gives?

METR thinks the time horizon metric helps resolve this seeming paradox.

https://t.co/ABTTjeHEtT https://t.co/UKZncja2Ai

The time horizon metric is meant to improve on existing benchmarks, which usually weren't selected to measure what we actually care about (will this thing save me time/money/labor?). Other benchmarks also often "saturate" in ~1 yr now, making it hard to do multiyear comparisons.

The oldest models tested, OpenAI's GPT-2 & 3, can't handle any tasks >1min. The newest, Anthropic's Claude Sonnet 3.7, was 50% successful at tasks that took expert programmers 59 min on avg.

Top models still typically fail at longer tasks.

Paper here: https://t.co/H0ekGAyvRH https://t.co/nHAzDvuijW

Based on this metric, AI progress speeding up. 2024-25 models x2 time horizon every ~3 months. The authors discuss reasons this new trend may not hold, but it's some of the strongest evidence yet that AI models have improved at economically valuable work at an accelerating rate. https://t.co/9C4UcTFrFq

Some reasons: Compute constraints might bite. Improvements to model agency might be a one-time boost. (Some cut in the other direction: devs might instead get a lot more juice out of agency improvements. AI could begin meaningfully automating AI research.) https://t.co/sYtrRV2J7D

Based on this metric, AI progress is speeding up. 2024-25 models x2 time horizon every ~3 months. The authors discuss reasons this new trend may not hold, but it's some of the strongest evidence yet that AI models have improved at economically valuable work at an accelerating… https://t.co/bxPo4Ok48c

Based on this metric, AI progress is speeding up. 2024-25 models x2 time horizon every ~3 months. The authors discuss reasons this new trend may not hold, but it's some of the strongest evidence yet that AI models have improved at economically valuable work at an accelerating rate.
Ofc, there are other reasons such an extrapolation might not work. The tasks METR chose might not generalize to real-world work. https://t.co/DxzHqbi85P

But at least in all of the robustness checks METR did, the time horizon growth trend was similar. Though AIs predictably perform worse on messier tasks and the time horizon shrinks by 5x if you raise the reliability threshold from 50% to 80%. https://t.co/cayVTih5X3

I think these large differences in what models can handle reliably will result in a 'jagged edge' of AI adoption, with checkable work that doesn't need to be right 100% of the time being automated far sooner than open-ended work with high stakes. https://t.co/cqsqmOZd1Z
These tasks are almost entirely new and unpublished, limiting the risk that the models are "contaminated," i.e. trained on the test data (tho some similar tasks likely appear in training data).

Perhaps the biggest limitation of this approach is the difficulty of creating another ~200 tasks for day-month long tasks.

Collecting this task data cost hundreds of thousands in contractor time alone. https://t.co/KzCjnpCDVm

This is something the authors are well-aware of, and in our conversations, they mentioned in-progress research to measure how much AI systems uplift human workers when they're randomly assigned to assist some and not others. https://t.co/6BvWMu25W6

Here's the piece again: https://t.co/ABTTjeHEtT

I don't trust our ability to assess the qualitative experience of animals we are raising to kill and eat. In general, moral cluelessness implies that we should not be confident in our beliefs, so IMO a lot of morality is about signaling to yourself and others that you have good values, which are more robust to changes in evidence/empirical understanding.

For Moore's Law? I've seen reports that it's starting to slow bc of physical limits.

The actual Turing Test has fallen.

GPT-4.5 is better at appearing human than we are.

When ChatGPT first came out, there were a ton of unsupported assertions that the Turing Test had been beaten. https://t.co/It8NSQ4FLV https://t.co/2E1N51nfwG

But when it was studied scientifically, humans were still better at appearing human than AIs, at least until July 2024, when a GPT-4 based model significantly outperformed humans at a 2-party Turing Test. https://t.co/mKo76qvVXR https://t.co/XJfndtuEAB

This new study actually uses Turing's original 3-party formulation (human "interrogator," and 2 witnesses, 1 AI and 1 human). Past studies used a 2-party approach (a judge talking to 1 witness and guessing if it's an AI). https://t.co/It8NSQ4FLV

Obviously, the Turing Test no longer feels like a sufficient test of truly general intelligence (i.e. AGI), but it was the most widely discussed measure of AGI for decades. In my Minds & Machines class in college we spent a long time on it & why it's actually a pretty good test.

So it's kinda weird that it came and went with so little fanfare!

You can also quibble with the specifics of whether this counts as a Real Turing Test, and a coauthor discusses that here: https://t.co/WLShMSXGJX

Anyway, you can try it for yourself here: https://t.co/woUxfMXGDp

And find the full preprint here: https://t.co/U8O3jP1mXV

Props to @camrobjones and Benjamin Bergen for actually testing this!

Frontier AI models are more capable than they've ever been, and they're being rushed out faster than ever. Not a great combination!

OpenAI used to give staff months to safety test. Now it's just days, per great reporting from @CristinaCriddle at the FT. 🧵 https://t.co/zSIqCd6RX7

Unsurprisingly, sources cite "competitive pressures" as the reason for the cuts. OpenAI's release of ChatGPT caused Google to declare a "code red" & change its risk tolerance. Gemini 2.5 Pro is arguably the most powerful model released & didn't have a safety report for weeks. https://t.co/IvKvBVRmBE
Props to @beafreyanolan and @jeremyakahn for reporting on Google's failure here in Fortune earlier this week: https://t.co/jt7HPKAMq1 https://t.co/BfhG1VT3if

Some people say, 'Who cares? These models clearly haven't taken over the world and aren't about to.'

While I agree we're not about to lose control of this gen of models, there's evidence that AI is getting closer to helping novices make bioweapons. https://t.co/069S8GBsUN https://t.co/hWhEMr4E3X

"Several of our biology evaluations indicate our models are on the cusp of being able to meaningfully help novices create known biological threats, which would cross our high risk threshold," reads OpenAI's DeepResearch system card https://t.co/Rsw0l3YDiZ https://t.co/jkvqrhz3nb

This system card, btw, came out weeks after the model was available and claimed that OpenAI was "intensifying [its] investment in safeguards," which seems contradicted by this new reporting. https://t.co/FPxR7SgjE5

Is there any principled reason to not test these new models as rigorously as older models?

No, it just takes time and money. https://t.co/O2WAsGWXk7

The leading AI companies have pre-deployment safety plans in which they commit to not release models deemed too risky. However, the pressure to downplay the risk until it's below these thresholds will make the speed pressure look quaint.

See this example I reported in the NYT:

https://t.co/JkOpAZTc9a https://t.co/ZtAZ7f6pbI

And the trend of taking safety less seriously as models get more capable should give us all pause, especially when voluntary commitments (which OpenAI and GDM have each recently broken) are how these companies are regulated.

If and when models actually are on the verge of doing really catastrophic stuff, the competitive pressures will be even more intense. There will be more business value at stake, more investor money on the line, and greater geopolitical tension over AI.

So if the voluntary guardrails aren't holding now, you can bet they won't hold then.
