🚨BREAKING🚨 OpenAI's top official for catastrophic risk, Joaquin Quiñonero Candela, quietly stepped down weeks ago — the latest major shakeup in the company's safety leadership. I dug into what happened and what it means for Obsolete 🧵 https://t.co/GWZIqOVbzw

Candela, who led the Preparedness team since July, announced on LinkedIn he's now an "intern" on a healthcare team at OpenAI.

A company spokesperson told me Candela was involved in the successor framework but is now "focusing on different areas."

https://t.co/MZwU7JFR9L https://t.co/4Vp4bWoVZC

This marks the second unannounced leadership change for the Preparedness team in less than a year. Candela took over after Aleksander Mądry was quietly reassigned last July — just days before Senators wrote to Sam Altman about safety concerns. https://t.co/Qrl5eAaUAQ https://t.co/97XWNUhV16

Candela's departure is part of a much larger trend. OpenAI has seen an exodus of top safety talent over the past year, including cofounder John Schulman, safety lead Lilian Weng, Superalignment leads Ilya Sutskever & Jan Leike, and AGI readiness advisor Miles Brundage. https://t.co/kiGRwfWjGg

When Leike left, he publicly stated that "safety culture and processes have taken a backseat to shiny products" at OpenAI. Miles Brundage cited publishing constraints and warned that no company is ready for artificial general intelligence (AGI). https://t.co/ttjz2G3H2E

With key safety leaders gone, OpenAI's formal governance is crucial but increasingly opaque. Key members of the board's Safety and Security Committee are gone, and the members of a new internal "Safety Advisory Group" (SAG) haven't been publicly identified.

An OpenAI spox told me that Sandhini Agarwal has been leading the safety group for 2 months, but that information hadn't been announced or previously reported. Given how much OpenAI has historically emphasized AI safety, shouldn't we know who is in charge of it? https://t.co/gjXzNGX9OD

A former employee wrote to me, “Even while working at OpenAI, details about safety procedures were very siloed. I could never really tell what we had promised, if we had done it, or who was working on it.”

These changes come amid growing concerns — reports of slashed testing times (which OpenAI pushed back on), the release of GPT-4.1 without a safety report, and a former researcher calling out the company's quietly reduced safety commitments. https://t.co/aNoduOQJ3A

This pattern isn't unique to OpenAI. Google still hasn't published a safety report for Gemini 2.5 Pro, arguably the most capable model available, in likely violation of the company's voluntary commitments.
The trend is the opposite of what you'd want to see! As models get more powerful and autonomous, companies race harder to beat each other to market, cutting corners along the way.

And when lawmakers have tried to regulate the industry, the companies have lobbied (quite successfully) to stop them.

https://t.co/Wbj7yUpYH7 https://t.co/YFeZjTixzB

There's a lot more in the full piece, check it out: https://t.co/MZwU7JFR9L

New reporting: OpenAI recently announced a nonprofit commission to guide how it spends its "potentially historic financial resources," but critics see it as a ploy to smooth its path to becoming fully for-profit, shedding the nonprofit control that was core to its founding. https://t.co/E8mwSuJMG9

"The story of OpenAI's history is trying to balance the desires to raise capital and build the tech and stay true to its mission," a former employee told me. The plan to fully convert to a for-profit, they argue, is an attempt to "separate these things."
https://t.co/ZnnTO4h7UQ

The commissioners were announced on Tuesday and includes a who's who of powerful California govt, civil society, and private sector figures, including the 95 y/o Dolores Huerta, who cofounded United Farm Workers with Cesar Chavez. https://t.co/PpUcSuPRLG

While OpenAI frames this as a philanthropic initiative, former employees say it's "pandering" — a blatant attempt to "bribe" California's nonprofit sector that might oppose their conversion, especially as the state AG has power to challenge it. https://t.co/trB8N5whmS

The legal hurdles are significant. The conversion faces Musk's lawsuit (now headed to trial), potential AG challenges, and now a new amicus brief from 12 former OpenAI employees who feel betrayed by the company abandoning its nonprofit roots.
Todor Markov, who left OpenAI, told me that the nonprofit commission "does nothing to alleviate the substantial concerns we raise in our amicus brief." https://t.co/EKMbR7CSob

The commission announcement came as OpenAI released o3, its most capable model yet — underscoring the immense value and risk embodied in the technology the nonprofit currently oversees — and the very control it's being asked to relinquish.
Some experts don't think there's a possible price on what the nonprofit would be giving up, given its charter to ensure that AGI is developed safely. https://t.co/qDjhxTcH2k

I loved this from a former employee: "Imagine you are a nonprofit whose mission is to ensure nuclear technology benefits humanity. And you literally have a controlling interest in the Manhattan Project. And it's 1943. For what amount of money should you sell that interest?"

All this amid an ongoing exodus of safety staff and reports of rushed safety testing. This week, I reported that Joaquin Quiñonero Candela quietly stepped down from leading catastrophic risk work — the latest shakeup in a year of high-profile safety exits.
https://t.co/MZwU7JFR9L https://t.co/4Rj1gpqcjL

OpenAI faces tight deadlines: if it doesn't convert by end of 2025, it must return $20B of recently raised funds. But as UCLA's Michael Dorff notes, "The only way to meet that deadline that I can see is for OpenAI to settle with everybody."

A former employee summed it up neatly: "I view what's happening now is: the profit motive's winning. They have given up on the altruistic angle. They've given up on trying to be the good guy, and they just want to win."

Genuinely shocked at this news. I've been covering OpenAI's efforts to shed its nonprofit controls since October & spoken to lots of experts. The plan was legally fraught and opposed by powerful interests, but it was hard not to feel that OAI would just get its way 🧵 https://t.co/0AsvX83I8l

CNBC article: https://t.co/30vJV3cHw2

OpenAI board chair Bret Taylor explains:

https://t.co/ZZVFSbalS2

(More analysis soon) https://t.co/wUNcCzqySb

Biggest Qs: 
1. What does this mean for investors? OAI reportedly gave investors in its last two rounds the ability to clawback $26.6B (+ interest) if it didn't restructure as a for-profit. This doesn't appear to be explicitly addressed in the blog post.

2. What is happening to the profit caps? This part of Sam's letter makes it seem like they might be changed/gone. Investors have agreed to caps on how much they can profit from their investment in OAI, with the money above the cap going back to the nonprofit. https://t.co/xE519efcZz
These caps vary, but are as high as 100x initial investment, which would require OAI to be one of the most valuable/profitable companies in the world to hit.
They have often been dismissed as NBD bc of this, but in a world where OAI's valuation is ~$300B and doubled in less than a year, they don't seem quite as theoretical.
3. What does this mean? OpenAI's nonprofit mission is to ensure AGI benefits all of humanity. It can't change that mission without a REALLY good reason. https://t.co/loWR9swByr

The nonprofit hasn't really done much grantmaking historically, but this graf+the nonprofit commission make me think they're planning to use the nonprofit to give away OAI products to social sector and govt groups

More context here: https://t.co/4V5jj5Ikxw

4. What went down in the meetings between OAI and the CA and DE AGs? They had the power to effectively block this restructuring and there was a strong legal case for doing so, but it was never just a legal question, but one of politics as well. https://t.co/MEbfIImYNt

5. If the nonprofit is indeed giving up the profit caps, how much is it being compensated for doing so? In other words, what is the expected value of OAI's future profits? The caps were justified by the idea that the first to build AGI would be able to effectively print money https://t.co/ymWbFC19fP

But it seems from this graf that OAI is going to argue that many places will build AGI, so no 1 company is going to pull in $1T+ profits, so the nonprofit is actually giving up less in expectation and will get less $ for the removal of the caps.

6. How much does the nonprofit control really matter if it's just going to act like a normal for-profit board? A q I was asked about this recently was effectively: why does this restructuring matter if the current board won't rein in the CEO/for-profit entity?

This is a totally good+fair Q! I think the profit caps matter, bc if OAI actually builds AGI, it's not crazy to think it can pull in $1T+ profits, and it would be a bummer if there were no legal obligation to share that money with the world.

And I think it matters if the directors can justify a decision that is good for the world/safety but bad for the bottom line. Keeping the nonprofit in control makes it a lot easier for that to happen.

When Sam was fired, OAI legal reportedly threatened to sue a director for breaching their duty to investors, but that actually wasn't their duty! Their duty was to humanity writ large. It may sound silly but I can imagine situations where those degrees of freedom really matter.

Anyway, I'm sure we'll learn a lot more soon.

1. I think the profit caps will be gone
2. I think OAI won't have to pay back the $26.6B to investors (bc they signed off on this in exchange for profit caps to be gone). They might pay some back, but not all.

3. I think nonprofit will be compensated 10s of billions for the removal of the caps
4. I think the nonprofit will largely use that money to buy OAI services for nonprofits and govts (targeting constituencies that can make life difficult for it, like CA nonprofits)
But these are based on reading between the lines of the announcement + my past reporting.

Last week, OpenAI sent a letter to the CA Attorney General defending its latest restructuring attempt. I got my hands on it and wrote up the whole story for Obsolete. Here are some of the most interesting+newsworthy parts 🧵 https://t.co/4zDvcavybM https://t.co/7Dwyi2xrRL

OAI says its unusual corporate governance deterred many potential investors — in tension with past Bloomberg reporting that the company's October fundraising round was oversubscribed.

Story: https://t.co/qSxuGp7y5T
Letter: https://t.co/LFkpGDokM3 https://t.co/21f52n934g

It wasn't clear if the nonprofit board would be able to fire directors on the new public benefit corporation (PBC) board — a key power. The letter confirms the nonprofit will have this power in the new plan. https://t.co/7i03Knyjqp
The nonprofit currently owns OpenAI's IP. The letter suggests that this ownership will transfer to the new PBC. https://t.co/032Ro6BIgD

Besides these newsworthy details, the vibe of the letter is worth commenting on. It's ostensibly written in response to a coalition of 50+ CA-based nonprofits that criticized OpenAI's previous restructuring effort. https://t.co/p9BFfgfv8Z

But it spends way more time talking about Elon Musk, whose name appears nearly 20 times throughout. This looks like a strategy to delegitimize critics of OpenAI's plans as self-interested competitors or Musk's unwitting lackeys. https://t.co/6XUWJp2vJf

This doesn't seem to be going over well with at least some members of the CA coalition. One of the leaders forwarded me the letter and an email from OpenAI representatives that reads very differently. https://t.co/gn9Eh2MeH6

OpenAI's lawyers also attack the coalition for daring to suggest that money could possibly have played a role in the decisions of employees to support Sam Altman after he was fired in Nov 2023. His firing jeopardized an $86B tender sale of employees' stakes in company profits. https://t.co/Uofnvzm5sA

The lawyers say that there is "zero support for that outrageous claim," but a former employee who signed the Altman reinstatement letter thinks otherwise, "That's bullshit and a blatant lie," they wrote to me. There were many conversations about "how does this affect the tender." https://t.co/1NEs5OWfkf

The funniest part: the letter represents the nonprofit board as deeply involved in the process, but then misspells the names of two of the directors! (Should be Ogunlesi and Hellmann.) https://t.co/6RZqdgx76F

Finally, OpenAI's letter frames the new restructuring plan as not a meaningful change to the level of control the nonprofit currently has. But by default, the nonprofit will go from total control, to a far lower level of control through owning a controlling stake in the new PBC. https://t.co/m1kn2SXgQI

The nonprofit can do things to recreate something like its current level of control, but it hasn't said that it will (aside from now promising the power to fire PBC directors). The Not for Private Gain project spells out some ideas for what this could look like. https://t.co/yNUewiWXSa

By default, the safeguards present in the status quo structure go away.
(Table and above ideas from Not for Private Gain project: https://t.co/0AP6aXrrY1) https://t.co/KG1EqsfNzO

I think the most telling thing about the new plan is what investors are doing and saying about it. SoftBank put in $30B with the right to clawback $10B if the restructuring didn't move forward by year-end.

SoftBank, at least, is pleased with the new PBC restructuring plan.

SoftBank's finance chief recently said of OpenAI's new proposal: "nothing has really changed."

https://t.co/8SXGlilcM1

SCOOP: Anthropic just released its most advanced AI model ever.

More to say soon, but check out the full piece here: https://t.co/duf5EOWTT1
@MaskedTorah I was referring to the version history on the website in the original piece. Currently thinking through how to clarify.

I'm working on an update to clarify this point. 

FWIW, I do think "quietly" is a fair description for the change. It only exists on page 18 of a PDF, and could have easily been mentioned on the webpage itself (which is what the Anthropic spokesperson sent to me). 

The original language includes "commit" which is equivalent to a promise.
@CRSegerie tysm!

Claude 4 Opus is the first Anthropic model to trigger AI safety level 3 protections after evaluators found it may help novices build bioweapons. Anthropic's chief scientist told TIME: "You could try to synthesize something like COVID or a more dangerous version of the flu."

In 2023, Anthropic promised it wouldn't release an ASL-3 model until it had defined ASL-4 standards and evaluations. But the company's latest safety framework includes only brief definitions of ASL-4 and punts on evals — despite treating Claude 4 Opus as ASL-3.

When I asked about this, Anthropic said the 2023 commitment was "outdated" and pointed to an October 2024 revision. The company now says it maps safety levels to increasingly stringent measures rather than requiring pre-defined future standards.

But what does a voluntary commitment mean if a company can decide to change its terms? This reveals the core problem with AI self-regulation — companies are essentially policing themselves with no external enforcement.

Anthropic told me that this change was approved by the company's Long Term Benefit Trust (LTBT), which appoints Anthropic's directors. This prompted me to look into how that was going, and it turns out the LTBT was supposed to have appointed 3 of 5 directors by now... https://t.co/5EeK0fe71l

but there are only 4 directors listed on Anthropic's website. 

In the absence of binding regulations, corporate governance ends up being really important. 

And now even the AI company most dedicated to safety is backpedaling on its commitments.

This calls to mind a conversation I had in Feb 2024 with a senior safety researcher at a top AI company, who told me that voluntary governance works, until you get close to artificial general intelligence (AGI), at which point competitive pressures become too strong.

A year and change later, those pressures appear to be winning. Insiders are making more concrete claims about the imminence of AGI. Meanwhile, safety practices at top companies appear to be suffering. https://t.co/rNBNIEijE4

Why does any of this matter? Well, Anthropic found that people with access to Claude 4 Opus were 2.5x more effective at acquiring a bioweapon than those with access to just the internet. 
https://t.co/Xx1HmNZ5X7 https://t.co/n1RkY3fbl1
The depth of these evaluations and the decision to classify Claude 4 Opus as ASL-3 out of abundance of caution are commendable, and Anthropic appears to be behaving more responsibly than its peers.
But it also could have been more transparent about the changes it made to its past commitments — the very mutability of which really stretches the meaning of "commitment."

New development in the OpenAI restructuring plan in WSJ from @berber_jin1 and @cdriebusch

Basically, a key regulator has hired a bank to assess the likelihood that OpenAI builds AGI.

Okay, not exactly, but kind of. 🧵 https://t.co/VvZVIikGWz

Right now, the nonprofit gets all the profit above various caps. OpenAI's plan would do away with those caps. So the nonprofit needs to be compensated for what it's losing, which requires an assessment of the net present value of all of OpenAI's future profits.

This implicitly requires the bank to assign probabilities to the likelihood of different outcomes, ranging from OpenAI going out of business to OpenAI building superintelligent AI that automates most human labor.

Gift link: https://t.co/6N8qLRyRwB

(There's a case that they should also consider the possibility OAI succeeds but the superintelligence kills everyone. But it would probably be wildly profitable right up until that happens, making the profit caps still bite.)

(I'm now realizing that any net present value calculation implicitly puts some probability on the world ending, but it's probably effectively 0.)

But anyway, I think my original framing is only a slightly tortured assessment of what's actually happening.

Overall, I think this development makes it more likely that the nonprofit gets more money for what it's giving up, though there's a lot more at stake to the restructuring than just profit caps, as I've written here: https://t.co/qSxuGp7y5T

State Senator Scott Wiener, author of California AI safety bill SB 1047, is back at it. He's been advancing a new bill, SB 53, to create whistleblower protections for AI employees. Wiener just amended it to include transparency requirements with TBD penalties from the CA AG 🧵 https://t.co/IgJIaHx2hj

Overall it's similar to SB 1047. The key diff? No liability provision, which is likely the thing industry hated the most. Some supporters of 1047 prev told me that its transparency provisions — e.g. requiring large AI cos to publish safety plans — were the most significant parts. https://t.co/hqRbFvGA1w

Others told me the whistleblower protections were most important. Well SB 53 now has both! It also follows recommendations from a working group convened by Gov. Newsom around when he vetoed 1047, making it more awkward for him to veto SB 53. Full report: https://t.co/HTpRraVvKF https://t.co/dkiChAexBX

That working group was chaired by Fei-Fei Li, whose opposition to SB 1047 was cited prominently in Nancy Pelosi's opposition statement. Li is close with Marc Andreessen (see below), so I was surprised at the report's openness to ANY regulation. https://t.co/fqUapkuMhF https://t.co/JDypkGMp10

(That report btw has 53 pages. Coincidence? Yes, probably.)

SB 53 has received 0 no votes so far, so my guess is that Wiener and team saw an oppo to go for something more ambitious without adding anything that would make Newsom too likely to veto.

https://t.co/6TAVSWhLBD https://t.co/x6hLwZdsqa

Enforcement? The state AG can bring civil penalties for violations. Amounts TBD.

Full text here: https://t.co/OEzbzmmEAe https://t.co/6ZjxtZwTYo

Even if the legislature voted with a veto-proof majority, they would still not override Newsom's veto, as that hasn't happened in CA for >40 years.

So the fate of SB 53 will once again come down to Newsom. I think that it has a much better chance of becoming law than 1047 did.

Why? Companies really don't want to be liable for harms they cause — AI companies maybe even more so, but this bill doesn't use liability. As mentioned, it would look bad for Newsom to veto something that was essentially recommended by his own working group.

I'd also be surprised if SB 53 gets anywhere near the critical attention 1047 got. I think the recent failed effort to ban all state AI regs for 10 years advanced by much of the industry has also made it harder for Dems to oppose ANY AI regulation. More: https://t.co/ew0CZ0U624

Original article by @shiringhaffary https://t.co/E31oCJ308Q

I haven't read the full paper yet, but the idea strikes me as more sensible than regulating at the model level. Only a handful of companies will be able to afford training frontier AI models. 
Paper link: https://t.co/Mgq2NCgQTC

If you think frontier AI is the source of biggest risks, then targeting the devs is smart. Compute thresholds are a crude proxy for model capability. I've also become convinced that there's a lot of risk in pre-deployment, esp as models get closer to automating AI research.

E.g. if OpenAI o6 is actually really capable + dangerous, it'll probably be used internally at OpenAI for a while before it's released. But an actually smart model wouldn't need to be publicly available to pose severe risks.
I don't have strong views yet on how much entity-based regulation mitigates pre-deployment risk, but it seems like a step in the right direction.

Important addendum! The updated bill targets large AI developers themselves, rather than models above compute thresholds. OpenAI's official "Our structure" webpage has been misstating the company's mission since at least May 2023.

The actual mission "is to ensure that artificial general intelligence (AGI)...benefits all of humanity."

A normal person may ask, who cares? https://t.co/zbFmq63sbl

Well, that "ensure" was something some early employees fought hard for. It allows the nonprofit board to do weird things like join another project if it's better positioned to build AGI — to ensure it's done safely. This mission statement also legally binds the nonprofit. https://t.co/exwBmDfMvj

That mission could actually have a material effect on how and whether OpenAI's for-profit restructuring goes through, as I've written about extensively:

https://t.co/r0gVwk1Mup
https://t.co/fBV2tHThuM https://t.co/7ttltJPlZV

OpenAI's "Our structure" webpage is not a legal document, and doesn't do anything to make the company's actual mission any less legally binding. But it does signal how far OpenAI has strayed from its founding ideals — at least 6mo before Sam Altman was fired.

h/t to Peter Barnett for catching this: https://t.co/cYz62M09L3

Anthropic could be bankrupted within the next few months, thanks to last week's barely covered legal ruling, which exposes the AI startup to billions to hundreds of billions in damages for its use of pirated, copyright-protected works. https://t.co/7dDiHU8FtH

Bizarrely, no mainstream outlet had yet covered this possibility, so I wrote it up for Obsolete. A judge certified a class action representing up to 7 million copyright-protected books that Anthropic pirated.

https://t.co/A5H0WVPJ8k https://t.co/KIjnwvT0bM

The judge has basically determined infringement took place, so the main thing left to be decided is the amount of damages, based on how many books are covered (likely 2M-5M) and the penalty-per ($750-$150,000), *overall $1.5B to $750B in damages.* https://t.co/ufzNwtyIcT

The latter won't actually happen, but a multibillion dollar judgment is conceivable. Could Anthropic survive a hypothetical $3B hit? Maybe, but it's a lot of money, and the company has the least access to capital of its peers. https://t.co/aI8DpuzK1I

Anthropic could settle, but doing so could still cost billions, given the ludicrous ceiling on damages. A spox told Obsolete it was “exploring all avenues for review.”

Yesterday, the company asked the judge to pause the case, but a knowledgeable friend thinks that's unlikely. https://t.co/gjWyhXJzPr

As I was finishing this piece, I realized it might help explain CEO Dario Amodei's about-face on taking money from Gulf dictators. @kyliebytes broke a great story about a memo Amodei sent Sunday announcing the decision... just days after the class action certification. https://t.co/NDzjlJC3yH

In some sense, Anthropic is unlucky to be first. But it also screwed up big-time in its use of pirated material for things that were not training, and thus don't qualify as fair use, per the judge's ruling. https://t.co/aVKHohlj1M

The big irony: Anthropic really did try harder than Meta to pay something for the books they used. In 2024, Anthropic began buying, scanning, and pulping books en-masse. Meta, with all the money in the world, never bothered (Zuckerberg also approved the piracy, per int. emails). https://t.co/x4KdmAgfRK

There's a lot more in the piece, so please do check it out! Thanks to my friend Vivian for the inspiration and input and to Sid Mahanta for the edits.

Artificial general intelligence is not inevitable.

My latest for The Guardian challenges one of the most popular claims made about AGI.

Among those who believe AGI is possible, it's common to think it's unstoppable, whether you're excited or terrified of the prospect 🧵 https://t.co/hMyADmgs4f

For instance, Sam Altman loves to invoke this idea, esp. when he's trying to compare himself to Oppenheimer. He's also said that AI could drive humanity extinct (he's stopped saying this as of late, but I think he still believes it).
https://t.co/8T4LMu5XsL https://t.co/eFW0sCok4a

So why would you build something that could lead to human extinction? Well, if it's going to happen anyway, better to be me than someone else who will be less responsible. This is the fundamental logic driving the AI race. It's what motivated DeepMind, OpenAI, Anthropic, etc. https://t.co/oNiiFng7XU

The people saying this often don't seem to know much about history. Even nuclear weapons, often given as an example of this, are far more contingent than most realize. Many doubted their feasibility well into the 40s, and the specter of a Nazi bomb was a big motivator. https://t.co/6dDt8MbzSq

Advocacy and organizing can have major effects on policymakers — another thing these tech elites don't want you to know. Reagan and Gorbachev nearly ditched ALL their nukes, in large part because of the Nuclear Freeze movement! https://t.co/7xtWUe8WZz

A point that should be made more often: govts have far less of an incentive to build AGI than businesses. They care about many more things than profitability. If govts get into the AGI game properly, it will be in large part be bc they were dragged there by AGI companies. https://t.co/4zT26sSdVz

The biggest irony of this argument is that, if it's true, why bother making it? I think many of the inevitabilists are trying to create a self-fulfilling prophecy. E/accs say acceleration is an inevitable consequence of thermodynamics, but they also are terrified of AI regs 🤔 https://t.co/kXyPzv1lO0

Many opponents of regulation say that it's not possible to truly control something like AI R&D. But creating frontier AI models is far closer to building nukes than to developing an iPhone app. This creates oppos for international regulation without Big Brother. https://t.co/Z6avkqm5o9

The world got together and fixed the ozone layer, has made serious and effective agreements around nukes, and has started to make progress (but not enough) on greenhouse gases. Some of this work is being undone, but it still shows that coordination is possible. https://t.co/m4oMeyYdhm

Anatomy of an infamous o3 hallucination.

I have a big piece on AI scheming coming out tomorrow, and my editor asked me if the conference o3 claimed to attend in 2018 was actually real.

Turns out the answer is: probably! But it gets a lot weirder. Short 🧵 https://t.co/xGoL2MirQz https://t.co/v13fRF5S6z

So this is the likeliest candidate for the panel discussion, and David Bray is probably the "source" o3 uses. Why? Because o3 says that the source was a former NPR staffer. I searched each panelist + NPR and Bray got the best hit. Turns out he directed a Tiny Desk episode. https://t.co/sp0Civw6op

Which is a surprising thing to do for a think tank executive. Less surprising? There's another David Bray who was a director with the Blue Man Group, which got him a 2009 Tiny Desk credit.

Now we don't know for sure that this is what o3 was referencing, but it seems likely. https://t.co/W5X7rxX4oD

The brazenness of this lie was enough for it to go viral, but it also shows how o3 can be an idiot savant. How many humans wouldn't double check the finding that this think tank dude also directed a Blue Man Group?

But the fact that the panel was probably real (and who knows, maybe someone really did say the thing o3 quoted at the panel) shows how insidious AI hallucinations can be. The models are generating plausible responses that have probably gotten more plausible as models got smarter.

Anyway, it was fun to get to the bottom of this, and I'm really excited to share my piece tomorrow! In the meantime, you should subscribe to Obsolete, where I write on the economics and geopolitics of the race to build machine superintelligence: https://t.co/fwg47QxavA

Would a chatbot kill you if it got the chance? It seems that the answer — under the right circumstances — is probably.

I wrote this week’s Bloomberg Weekend Essay. I get into the alarming rise of AI scheming — blackmail, deceit, hacking, and, in some extreme cases, murder 🧵 https://t.co/4D0TpuNBil

Researchers have been putting AIs in scenarios where they face a choice: obey safety protocols, or act to preserve themselves — even if it means letting someone die.

This is only possible bc AIs have gotten smarter and more agentic.

🎁 Gift link:
https://t.co/94ctdVn52H https://t.co/Q4pS3tF9UD

These smarter AIs are better at understanding what we want, making them more useful. But they are better at scheming against us and may also be more likely to do so in the first place. https://t.co/uYg3qY9RDP
Are smarter models more likely to scheme? Early signs point to yes, but it may be too soon to say. https://t.co/FuHlZNMHPL
Why does this happen? AIs are increasingly being trained to win at real-world tasks, and sometimes cheating and lying help you win. https://t.co/tA6s0IZsCG

This may best explained by the return of reinforcement learning (RL). Classic stories of AIs doing bizarre things to maximize their reward — e.g. a video game boat spinning in circles instead of finishing the race — are a consequence of RL.

These experiments may be some early evidence for the idea that for almost any goal, staying alive+gaining power emerge as subgoals. This idea, "instrumental convergence," has long been theorized by AI safety researchers as perhaps the main reason we'd lose control of AI. https://t.co/8Aeaz8OcFw

We should think of these AIs as "increasingly smart sociopaths" says scheming researcher Jeffrey Ladish, whose research org Palisade found that OpenAI's o3 would routinely hack its way to a win at chess and also refuse to let itself be shut down. https://t.co/HEzhRAYMI9

Anthropic made waves with its finding that Claude would blackmail someone to prevent itself from being shut down, but it turns out every leading AI company had a model that would do the same the vast majority of the time. https://t.co/dOSHCF0ivp

So what? You can get chatbots to say basically anything, and these researchers are highly motivated to do so (Trump AI Czar David Sacks voiced this skepticism).

The researchers acknowledge many of these scenarios are unrealistic but think they're useful leading indicators. https://t.co/eHckSyvR61 https://t.co/FYBOxbwsJF

Are they? Or are they making the same mistake as ape language researchers, desperate to confirm their beliefs?

My sources welcomed this critique and pointed out the efforts they made to control their experiments and open source their methods.

https://t.co/VROT8mQa7S https://t.co/EmMakdXHiC

Also, one big difference: apes aren't rapidly getting better at acquiring human language.

AIs aren't currently capable of the long-term planning required to engage in catastrophically dangerous scheming bc they're not capable of *any* long-term planning. https://t.co/Uh61kU5Hn1

But that is changing at an exponential rate, across a large variety of domains, so any safety strategy that relies on AIs being incapable of long-term planning is not robust to continued capability gains.

https://t.co/GSBvTtWyyt https://t.co/Jz4KwMWl0O
Oh also AIs are getting smart enough to clock when they're being tested, which might break the main way we evaluate whether they're safe. Oops. https://t.co/zaQ66Lhg1c
So what are the stakes here? It really depends on how capable AIs get. But they range from: unreliable cheating chatbots all the way to human extinction. https://t.co/L1MeGb5utI

What would be most worrying? A fully automated AI company, says Ryan Greenblatt, whose influential research on Alignment Faking found AIs would appear more cooperative to avoid having their values changed. https://t.co/trH5AJdraO

But who would want to automate AI R&D?

Nearly every AI CEO?

GPT-5 reaction 🧵

If I could only see one AI benchmark score, this would be it. AI evals nonprofit METR measured how long it took human programmers to complete 80+ tasks to come up with a time horizon metric for AI models. GPT-5 is the new SOTA at 2.29hrs https://t.co/eaYOkiHwTf

METR found that this metric is doubling every ~7 months since 2019, with an acceleration since 2024. 2.29hrs is impressive but below some expectations:
https://t.co/OB8RACCzMQ

I wrote it up for Nature here:
https://t.co/ad1tj9OTAG

METR's post:
https://t.co/rfqUyJ5NAv

Based on the benchmarks presented+demo, GPT-5 seems like it will be a noticeable improvement on most use cases, but scores about the same or worse than Claude on coding.

If it's actually way less likely to hallucinate, as OpenAI claims, that will be a big deal. https://t.co/qYE3ozx5Gm

It will also be available to free users, which I think will have a huge effect. The vast majority of ChatGPT users don't pay for it, and I know a lot of people who have been underwhelmed by AI capabilities but were basing that off of using the free version.

This is a loss leader and means of getting new customers for OAI, ofc, but it is also consistent with the philosophy of preparing society through iterated deployment. If you haven't used anything more capable than 4o, 5 will probably feel like a big step up.
Ofc, in OpenAI tradition, the link to "Try on ChatGPT" doesn't actually work yet. smdh.

OAI says GPT-5 is a lot less deceptive than o3 (while tacitly admitting that o3 was extremely deceptive. Given that 5 seems to be a kind of amalgamation of different models, I'd guess they at least have some kind of classifier checking outputs for deception. https://t.co/e7yXsqci5r

OAI says they aren't training on the chain of thought, which is a good norm to spread. Training on CoT can cause it to become less faithful/trustworthy.

Random fun discovery from METR CEO Beth Barnes — one of the examples they demoed is just wrong lol
https://t.co/W8PCo1WIYO

Which reminds me of this incredible find from Toby Ord — when OpenAI announced o3, they showed an example of it solving a brain teaser, but if you dig into the actual chain of thought, it was cheating. OAI CHOSE this example to display to the world.
https://t.co/FbC8xNnt1x https://t.co/cTNW98pmdO

So yeah, take all of these demos and benchmarks with grains of salt and use the thing for yourself.

Overall, lot more to dig into and hard to say how the public will react once they have it.

Really important dynamic and creates a frog-boiling situation.

A consensus is forming: GPT-5 means that AI is out of steam. The model is a big flop and barely better than rivals, if at all.

But it's just not true — our expectations have dramatically changed.

If GPT-4 came out today, it would be an expensive, slow, dumb embarrassment. https://t.co/egAkVJCVBu

But at the time, it shocked the world, along with AI insiders who had lost track of how fast the technology was progressing. We compared GPT-4 to GPT-3.5 and were amazed. There wasn't really anything else to compare it to!

https://t.co/JN8TlYkPQP https://t.co/d8oFyKp8Yc

But now, we compare GPT-5 to its many contemporaries and find only incremental improvements and decide that AI is hitting a wall.

This doesn't make much sense! https://t.co/LG1PcWmSaU

People seem to be forgetting what GPT-4 was actually like (OpenAI's horrendous naming practices are largely to blame here, given how many new and improved models they named GPT-4). https://t.co/jZDdGneP4n
This narrative is already having real-world effects and appears to be shaping major policy decisions. https://t.co/MJgcdUb6AV

The media is playing a big role in this. The FT cut a quote from @Miles_Brundage in half to make him seem to downplay AGI. This is the opposite of Miles' whole shtick! The FT changed it with no correction. https://t.co/rhj43i1wZb https://t.co/S7nVlmjxcC

We may never see another GPT-4 moment, which was the result of a perfect storm. If anyone actually manages to build AGI, they're incentivized to use it internally long before they release it to the world (which would be a bad situation!) https://t.co/BwnDyY7RO8

We're now in a frog-boiling situation, where advances are in technical domains that are illegible to the public or quickly metabolized as nothingburgers. But GPT-5 is the state-of-the-art on many things, including METR's time horizon survey. https://t.co/FqrhvLcOlm
METR has also done some of the best work showing the limitations of AI benchmarks, which appear to be capturing less real-world impact than previously thought. https://t.co/XBN4Emzwhu

Sam Altman says he wants to prepare the world for AGI through "iterative deployment." ChatGPT was a success on this front (though it also kicked off fierce competition and corner-cutting). https://t.co/wyiEnqzXs4

However, Altman and OpenAI bungled GPT-5's rollout so badly they're now giving people the false impression that AI is over. They could have done many things to avoid this. Oh well. https://t.co/0VsiPmPmMr

But after more reflection, I think the strategy of iterative deployment is intrinsically flawed. A defining feature of being human is the ability to get used to almost anything, which in this case could coincide with inching toward disaster. https://t.co/B4Dp60n0aF

text:
People should be more aware of the secret METR graph, where they plotted 9 other benchmarks the same way and found exponential progress on all of them. Absolute performance and the slope of the graph (i.e. doubling time) varied, but they were all exponential. 

Ofc, anything you can grade in this way is something AI can hill-climb on. Experts I've interviewed have disagreed on how different things that we don't have benchmarks for would look. Some think it'd be similar, just starting from smaller absolute values. Others think the slopes for what I'll call "unbenchmarkable" tasks would be a lot flatter, but still exponential. 

I think at this point, the preponderance of evidence points toward: if you can start making deep learning work on some domain, it will improve exponentially. This won't be true in every case, but appears to be closer to the default than not. 

And when AI is really bad at something (e.g. computer use), it may just be doubling from 4 second tasks to 8 seconds. But when you're in the minutes range, you start to see the progress very quickly. I think this explains agents working "all of a sudden" very nicely.

Some think it'd be similar, just starting from smaller absolute values. Others think the slopes for what I'll call "unbenchmarkable" tasks would be a lot flatter, but still exponential. 

I think at this point, the preponderance of evidence points toward: if you can start making deep learning work on some domain, it will improve exponentially. This won't be true in every case, but appears to be closer to the default than not. 

And when AI is really bad at something (e.g. computer use), it may just be doubling from 4 second tasks to 8 seconds. But when you're in the minutes range, you start to see the progress very quickly. I think this explains agents working "all of a sudden" very nicely.

text:
But with chip export controls, the Bernie+AOC proposal is actually the closest thing to a pause anyone in office is offering.

And the arguments about chip controls don't make sense. US leverage is eroding regardless of what the US does. China is already trying as hard as it can to catch up the frontier on AI chips, but experts think this is still years away. 

It's hard not to read this as a piece that was written about just the data center moratorium and then was insufficiently updated in light of the change to the proposal. For instance, it cites Bernie's February proposal when observing that he doesn't lead with x-risk. But his proposal with AOC last week opens with a bunch of quotes about job loss and x-risk: https://t.co/ReYlga5H9e

text: Writing a book is hard enough. Writing one about AI is tempting fate. But as fast as AI moves, the story of what's happening hasn't changed: a tiny group of people, backed by the best-resourced organizations in the world, is trying to render you obsolete—

text: Allegation that the OpenAI+a16z super PAC is violating campaign finance law: https://t.co/3BmJnesOZu

raw.full_text: Allegation that the OpenAI+a16z super PAC is violating campaign finance law: https://t.co/3BmJnesOZu

text:
There have been several high profile incidents of OpenAI and a16z's super PAC Leading the Future (and its affiliate Build American AI) engaging in pretty egregious astroturfing. But it's also notable that part of why we know about the astroturfing is that it was conducted in an incredibly sloppy and slapdash manner for an organization with effectively unlimited cash.

Some examples:

•  LTF by their own admission says a "third party vendor" they paid used a fake AI journalism platform "Acutus Wire" with stories written by bots, complete with a "Michael Chen" emailing apparently dozens of folks asking for comment and engagement on their AI written stories. This was found out by the Midas Project partially because the poorly coded Acutus website explicitly included a javascript file on the webpage which talked about "suggested questions for the *AI interviewer* to ask."

• The journalist @TaylorLorenz found out about a program where Build American AI paid up to $5,000 per Tik Tok video to promote its content w/o disclosing the source of the payment. Lorenz writes: "WIRED first learned about the campaign after this article’s author was invited by SM4 to participate." So to be clear — Build American AI's vendor reached out to a well known journalist asking her to participate in its astroturfing campaign.

• Campaign Legal Center recently filed a complaint to the FEC about Leading the Future potentially not complying with (pretty generous and relaxed!) election disclosure rules. In the complaint, it discusses the fact that Leading the Future's Democratic and Republican sub entities (Think Big and American Mission) paid the vast majority of their disbursements to LLCs created on the same day as one another, and do not appear to have any other clients. From the complaint: "Both LLCs were formed on the exact same day—October 30, 2025—and both identify their addresses as mail centers in Nevada. Neither LLC has a website or other digital footprint, nor do they appear to have other clients, despite being reported by Think Big and American Mission, respectively, as the payees for a full suite of consulting and advertising services... The overall record therefore indicates that these LLCs are not bona fide commercial vendors that provided the services attributed to them by Think Big and American Mission, but rather serve as payment clearinghouses for the super PACs to illegally conceal the true recipients of over $10.5 million in super PAC payments."

• To close with a small issue, but my personal favorite: Build American AI ran ads on this platform that said we should "Put People over Profit"  (a rather completely absurd thing for a massive anti regulation Super PAC to say). But in the (AI generated?) image they included for the ad about people over profit, it shows the money *outweighing* the people!

The combination of functionally infinite money and crippling incompetence is a bit surprising, but not something that will necessarily last.

As I wrote in my forthcoming book Obsolete (see bio): "The industry has a bottomless pit of money, which it uses to reward capitulation and punish resistance. But money is essentially its *only* advantage. AI’s reformers have the ideas, the principles, and the public sympathy. To overcome the money, we need to harden that sympathy into organization—to upend the political calculus, to make capitulation hurt worse than resistance."

text:
So the two options presented here by OpenAI employees are superintelligent systems:
1. That we can’t really understand or control, doing things that are hopefully what we would have wanted if we knew better
2. As genius advisers

I think way too many ai people put too much stock in what they would do with great advice, and way too little in what Stephen miller would do, even though the latter is way more relevant to what actually gets done with ASI! We should expect politicians and executives to use ASI to advance their existing goals, many of which are culture war nonsense, zero/negative sum fights, rent seeking, just much more effectively. 

In short, if your vision for positive futures with asi don’t account for power, they aren’t worth much. 

This is one of the core ideas i argue in my forthcoming book Obsolete (deets in bio) and I think it’s been a huge mistake ai safety can’t afford to keep making.

text:
Still parsing the Fable news, but my best guess as to what happened is:

Now that the stakes have ratcheted up, sr govt people are properly engaging for the first time with things that AI people take for granted—the models are blackboxes, there is no universal defense against jailbreaks—and going 'what the actual fuck?? this is unacceptable.'

The export controls are the closest thing to pulling the plug that the govt has, so they used it. Culture warriors like Hegseth are trying to take credit for it after the fact, but likely had nothing to do with the decision (hence the delay between action and gloating). I wouldn't be surprised if antipathy toward Anthropic/Dario and/or Brockman's $25M to Trump played a role in this, but idt they're the only or even the main reasons for this.

text:
I want to clarify that I never claimed that OpenAI is a profitable business, or even has a good or sustainable business model, but simply that this data falsifies a big claim that Zitron has repeatedly made. He seems to have moved the goal posts to claiming that training costs should count toward the gross margins, but that is a totally different argument. 

If you make money from your customers on average, you can grow your way to recouping even very high fixed costs. If you instead lose money from the typical customer, as Zitron repeatedly contended, then growing more will make you run out of money faster. This is an incredibly important distinction, and getting it wrong for so long (in the face of reporting from the Information, which Zitron favorably cites elsewhere, as well as independent estimates) is frankly disqualifying for a commentator on AI industry economics.

text: On July 4, 2025, a protester shot a police officer in the neck at a noise demonstration outside the Prairieland ICE detention center in Texas. Federal prosecutors used the incident to bring sweeping charges against anyone they could connect to the event—including a woman who was parked in her car half a mile away when she heard a gunshot. In March 2026, a jury convicted eight of providing material support to terrorists, the first such charges ever brought against alleged “antifa” members. While five were charged with attempted murder, only the shooter was convicted. The others were convicted on terrorism charges anyway, meaning the government was able to effectively criminalize common protest tactics, such as wearing black, using encrypted messaging apps, possessing radical zines, and, in open carry states like Texas, bringing guns. The husband of one of the protesters didn’t attend, but was convicted of concealing evidence—what libertarian Reason magazine called “constitutionally protected pamphlets”—and now faces up to 40 years in federal prison. A former Department of Justice domestic terrorism counsel called the prosecution “incredibly dangerous” and warned it would cause Americans to stop exercising their rights “for fear of being swept up in some kind of dragnet.”
